This means it's a wonderful day for those of you who sell, market, teach, act or write.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Quiet research done behind the scenes might yield financial information that can benefit you or boost your earnings. It might also help you wrap up old business. Settle old bills or disputes. Fortunately, with Venus in your house of communications, your words are golden.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Enjoy the company of friends, groups and organizations today. You have lots of energy to share, and others want to be in your company. It's a particularly good day to discuss your future goals with someone to get their feedback.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor William H. Macy (1950) shares your birthday. You're courageous, enthusiastic and fascinated by ideas and ideals. You have a strong curiosity about people and events. This year you will reap the benefits of all your hard work. You might attain power and leadership. Seize opportunities in business and act on them. Expect kudos and acknowledgment.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: White Sox prospect Sam Antonacci turning heads at WBC: 'He's made a tremendous impression'
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. — It has gotten somewhat lost in the aftermath of catcher <b>Kyle Teel’s </b>injury, but infield prospect<b> Sam Antonacci </b>has burst onto the scene with a productive spring for the White Sox and for Team Italy at the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>Antonacci, who hit a two-run home run in Italy’s 8-6 win over Team USA on Tuesday night, has been a key contributor this spring with the Sox, batting .308 in six games. Despite his bat-to-ball skills, however, Antonacci will start the season in the minors.</p><p>“He’s made a tremendous impression [with Team Italy], not surprised,” general manager <b>Chris Getz</b> said. “From the beginning when we signed him, the feedback we were getting from our player-development crew, his stops at different affiliates last year, this is a kid that can do a lot of different things to help a team win.</p><p>“What he’s about, what he brings on a daily basis, it’s a winning style of baseball that’s going to fit in well here. I’m confident that the South Side is going to enjoy watching Sam Antonacci when the time comes.”</p><p>Getz said the Italian coaching staff has enjoyed Antonacci’s competitiveness. He plays a hard-nosed, high-effort style a la <b>Chase Meidroth</b>.</p><p><b>Baldwin sidelined</b></p><p>Outfielder <b>Brooks Baldwin </b>had an MRI exam Tuesday for inflammation in his right elbow. He had been out of the lineup for most of the last week.</p><p>“I think it was something that popped up when he was throwing,” manager <b>Will Venable</b> said before the game Thursday. “It’s similar to what he felt last year. He’s felt this before, and as far as the exact timeline of when the pain came about, [that’s] better coming from him.”</p><p>Venable didn’t sound too optimistic about Baldwin being ready for Opening Day, which would open up a spot for <b>Tristan Peters</b>, <b>Derek Hill </b>or <b>Jarred Kelenic</b>.</p><p>Hill, a first-round pick of the Tigers in 2014, finished with five outs above average last season. He’s a plus runner and a good defender. The Sox’ outfield defense is a question mark at best, so Hill could be a consideration.</p><p>“Elite defense,” Venable said. “He’s a guy that goes out there and makes all the plays. He’s somebody that you know is able to do different things at the plate — lay down bunts, can move guys over. He’s been around for a little bit, but [he’s] still very much trying to establish himself. And I think part of that is just understanding his skill set. I think we’re seeing that this spring.”</p><p><b>Kay rounding into form</b></p><p>After signing a two-year, $12 million contract in the offseason, left-hander<b> Anthony Kay</b> isn’t fighting for a rotation spot. That certainty is a big boost as he gets reacclimated to a major-league schedule.</p><p>“It’s definitely been a little bit of a transition period because Japan was the seven-day schedule,” he said Wednesday. “It’s been really good; the training staff has been really good; the pitching coaches have been really good. We have some good communication in figuring out what we need to do.”</p><p><b>SOX 3, GIANTS 3</b></p><p><b>Derek Hill</b> hit a solo home run in the third inning, and <b>Lenyn Sosa </b>opened the scoring with a solo homer in the second inning.</p><p>• Right-hander <b>Jonathan Cannon</b> struggled with his command, allowing four walks in his fourth spring-training appearance. He pitched 3⅓ innings and allowed two runs and five hits.</p><p>• <b>On deck:</b> Cubs at Sox, 3:05 p.m. Friday, Glendale, <b>Riley Martin</b> vs.<b> Davis Martin</b>.</p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Woman injured in East Garfield Park shooting
entries.summary: <p>A woman was hospitalized after being shot Thursday evening in East Garfield Park, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The woman, 40, was inside a vehicle in the 3100 block of West Madison Avenue at 8:01 p.m. when she was shot in the calf, police said. She took herself to Mount Sinai Hospital where she was reported in critical condition.</p><p>No one is in custody.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Libertyville man accused of sending threatening letters to federal judges
entries.summary: <p>A Libertyville man has been charged with sending threatening letters to judges in Florida and Texas, according to the U.S. Attorney's Office.</p><p>James Lebuhn, 65, is charged with two counts of mailing a threatening communication, according to the U.S. attorney’s office.</p><p>He is accused of sending threatening letters to U.S. District Court judges in Amarillo, Texas, and Fort Pierce, Florida, though charging documents didn’t detail the threats made in the letters or when they were sent.</p><p>“The Chicago U.S. Attorney’s Office is committed to holding accountable anyone who seeks to intimidate and instill fear in members of the federal government, including the esteemed public servants on the bench,” said U.S. Attorney Andrew S. Boutros.</p><p>Lebuhn is set to appear in court March 26 in front of U.S. District Judge Sara Ellis.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: High school students ace early voting program
entries.summary: <p>High school students made voting history in suburban Cook County, and they did it in a single day.</p><p>Through the clerk’s new "Defenders of DA'mocracy" High School Early Voting Program, student-run early voting sites inside 24 suburban high schools recorded 1,136 ballots cast on Feb. 26. To put that in perspective, that single-day total surpassed the 1,021 ballots cast during the first seven days of early voting at our initial countywide locations.</p><p>This is more than a milestone — it is proof that when we bring civic engagement directly to young people, they respond.</p><p>Developed in partnership with the Chicago Bears, this first-of-its-kind initiative placed fully operational early voting sites inside participating high schools. More than 125 trained and certified student election judges administered official polling locations under the supervision of the Cook County clerk’s office. They handled voter check-in, secured ballots, operated voting equipment and followed the same strict chain-of-custody and security protocols used at every polling place across suburban Cook County.</p><p>This was not a simulation. It was democracy in action.</p><p>Research shows that when young people participate in their first eligible election, they are far more likely to remain engaged for years to come. By bringing early voting directly into high schools, we are removing barriers, building confidence and fostering lifelong civic participation at a formative moment.</p><p>The energy inside these schools was inspiring. Students were not only casting ballots, they were also leading, administering and safeguarding the democratic process. That is an investment in the future of our community that will pay dividends for years to come.</p><p>Expanded early voting continues across suburban Cook County, with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/01/early-voting-chicago-suburbs" >55 locations</a> open ahead of Tuesday's gubernatorial primary election. I encourage all eligible voters to take advantage of this opportunity and make their voices heard.</p><p>Democracy is strongest when participation is broad, inclusive and accessible — and our students have shown us exactly what that looks like.</p><p><i>Monica Gordon, Cook County clerk</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-500000" name="module-500000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Energy engagement </h3><p>Thank you for the two articles on important energy issues in the March 6 Sun-Times. The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2026/03/04/chicago-construction-clayco-solar-power-energy-business" >article </a>by Amy Yee — "Chicago-based construction firm Clayco launches solar energy business" — underlines the need for vastly more use of solar, as well as wind and geothermal power — all alternative power sources.</p><p>But another <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/springfield/2026/03/04/alternative-energy-suppliers-electricity-bills-reform-legislation" >article</a> in the same paper — "For alternative energy supplier customers who routinely overpay, proposed reforms could provide some help" — confuses the issue by calling some different providers "alternatives" to the traditional suppliers. These different providers including North American Power, may or may not supply energy from alternative sources. The difference is important, because alternative sources can provide energy without climate change, and for less. </p><p><i>Ann Boisclair, Glen Ellyn</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Night spent in separate beds was a revelation
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have been married 44 years and have always slept in the same bed with my wife. Last week, I caught a virus, and we decided that I would sleep in the spare bedroom until it was gone. I have now discovered I sleep better by myself, and I enjoy the freedom of the bed. How do I tell my wife I'd prefer sleeping alone? I love her to death, but I really like my freedom in bed. — <i>SLEEPING FOR ONE IN WISCONSIN</i></p><p><b>DEAR SLEEPING:</b> Couples sleep apart for many reasons — and it doesn't signal that there is anything wrong with the marriage. Tell your wife what a restful and restorative sleep you had in the spare room ("the best you've had in years"). A good night's sleep is imperative for good health. Then explain that it's nothing personal, but you would like to continue sleeping solo.</p><p>Don't be surprised if she then tells you that she, too, slept better not having to share the bed. However, if she isn't receptive, perhaps two beds in your main bedroom would work for the two of you.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My 71-year-old husband tells looonnnggg stories about himself to everyone — family, friends and even strangers. I'm bored to tears hearing the same stories over and over, and I can see others' eyes glaze over to the point of avoidance. I have asked him to share only stories that are less than 10 years old, but that hasn't stopped him. What else can I do? This has gotten so bad that I find myself avoiding social situations with him. — <i>MAKE IT STOP IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR MAKE IT STOP:</b> Has your husband always been like this, or could his repetitiveness be a symptom of mental decline? When people regale others with old stories, it can mean that their short-term memory is deteriorating. Is the problem that your husband had an illustrious career years ago but has nothing recent to discuss? If his problem is simply that he's a crashing bore, consider socializing without him more often. Your friends and family will appreciate you for it.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I live in an independent retirement home. There is a lady who sits at our table at lunch. If someone asks a question, before the person being addressed can respond, she butts in and answers. If someone says something, she tries to top them. We have tried insulting her, but she pouts for a few minutes and then is back at it. When we moved to another table, she moved also. Any suggestions? — <i>SPOILED LUNCH IN ALABAMA</i></p><p><b>DEAR SPOILED:</b> Yes, rather than shun the clueless woman, one of you should have a private conversation with her. (Is she in control of all of her faculties?) Explain that her personality is "too strong and dominant" for the rest of you, which has made you all uncomfortable, and this is why she should sit elsewhere at lunchtime. If she denies it, give her some examples. Do not expect her to like it, but if you speak up, you may be able to enjoy your lunch in peace after that. If not, talk to the manager of the retirement home about reassigning her.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: President Trump's economy is a failure of his own making
entries.summary: <p>As a candidate, President Donald Trump <a class="Link" href="https://newsroom.ap.org/editorial-photos-videos/detail?itemid=cf9f6cb9fad145bfa6626afc0f2deb33&mediatype=video" target="_blank" >promised</a> to "create millions and millions of jobs, massively raise wages for American workers, and make the United States into a manufacturing powerhouse like it used to be many years ago."</p><p>More than a year in, the Trump economy is an explicit failure, with conditions ominously reminiscent of the months leading up to the Great Recession of 2008. The difference is that the Great Recession was precipitated by a global financial crisis. The current downturn is the result of the administration’s own policy decisions, including tariffs, immigration restrictions and government workforce reductions.</p><p>From day one, the second Trump administration began destabilizing the labor market, presiding over a stunning collapse in federal employment. </p><p>The Department of Government Efficiency — charged with shrinking the federal workforce — oversaw the elimination of more than <a class="Link" href="https://www.hcamag.com/ca/specialization/payroll/us-job-cuts-surge-in-february-amid-federal-workforce-reductions/527698#:~:text=Industries%20facing%20biggest%20layoffs,job%20cuts%20through%20February%202024." target="_blank" >62,530 federal jobs</a> in just the first two months of 2025, a staggering 41,311%<b></b> increase in federal layoffs compared with the previous year, according to the website Human Resources Director, which looks at hiring trends. These cuts didn’t simply trim bureaucracy — they decimated entire agencies, hollowed out public services and sent shock waves through communities dependent on federal employment.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bc0000" name="module-bc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>The job losses did not stop in Washington, D.C. Federal contract cancellations and funding freezes created collateral damage, prompting layoffs across the private sector. Technology firms, nonprofits and service providers found themselves suddenly cut off from long-standing federal contracts and grants. Private employers cited the "DOGE impact" as the reason for 63,583 layoffs, showing clearly how reckless cuts spilled into the broader economy.</p><h3>Where’s the hiring boom?</h3><p>Rather than ushering in a hiring boom, the U.S. economy has racked up months of job losses. The nation lost a <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/economy/u-s-lost-92000-jobs-last-month-and-unemployment-rate-rises-to-4-4#:~:text=to%2D4%2D4-,U.S.%20lost%2092%2C000%20jobs%20last%20month%20and%20unemployment%20rate%20rises,could%20add%20to%20that%20uncertainty" target="_blank" >stunning 92,000 jobs </a>in February, while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%, with the increase entirely at the expense of Americans of color. </p><h3></h3><p>While the white unemployment rate remained unchanged at 3.7%, the Black, Latino and Asian rates all rose — with the Black rate more than double the white rate at 7.7%. With revisions showing a loss of 17,000 jobs in December, total job growth over the last 14 months has been <a class="Link" href="https://www.ms.now/rachel-maddow-show/maddowblog/team-trump-struggles-to-spin-on-the-worst-job-numbers-since-the-great-recession" target="_blank" >just 150,000</a> — a sharp contrast to the 1.74 million jobs added in the 14 months prior.</p><p>This is not the record of a president creating "millions and millions" of new jobs — it is the record of a labor market stifled by federal decisions.</p><p>Trump also promised to "massively raise wages," but here again, the results fall far short. To be sure, wages have risen — but only modestly. </p><p>Average hourly earnings increased<a class="Link" href="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/06/february-2026-jobs-report.html" target="_blank" > 3.8% year‑over‑year by this February</a>, and weekly wages rose 4.3% year‑over‑year. These gains are not insignificant, but they are nowhere near the sweeping wage transformation Trump promised. In fact, much of the improvement in real wages owes to falling inflation — down from 3% when Trump took office to 2.4% by January — rather than to rapid wage acceleration. That wage growth, such as it is, is largely offset by soaring costs: Electricity is up <a class="Link" href="https://www.foxbusiness.com/economy/americans-hit-soaring-electricity-bills-price-hikes-outpace-inflation-nationwide" target="_blank" >6.3%,</a> medical care is up <a class="Link" href="https://press.spglobal.com/2014-01-09-Healthcare-Expenditures-for-Commercial-Plans-up-3-2-in-the-Year-to-August-2013-S-P-Healthcare-Claims-Indices" target="_blank" >3.2%</a>, and housing <a class="Link" href="https://www.nerdwallet.com/finance/learn/rental-market-trends" target="_blank" >continues to rise</a>.</p><p>Nowhere is the distance between promise and performance greater than in manufacturing. Far from restoring America’s industrial might, manufacturing employment instead has declined. The sector lost 12,000 jobs in February alone, defying economists’ expectations of gains. Manufacturers shed <a class="Link" href="https://www.americanprogress.org/article/trumps-trade-war-squeezes-middle-class-manufacturing-employment/#:~:text=Manufacturing%20employment%20has%20been%20falling,by%2076%2C000%20and%2018%2C000%2C%20respectively." target="_blank" >42,000 jobs</a> between April 2025 and August 2025, and total manufacturing employment fell by 33,000 jobs in 2025 — hardly the profile of a “manufacturing powerhouse.”</p><h3>Tariff strategy misfires</h3><p>The administration’s tariffs strategy has massively backfired. John Deere reported <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2025/09/04/business/john-deere-tractor-sales-down-farmers-struggle.html" target="_blank" >$300 million in tariff‑related costs</a> before laying off hundreds of workers. Tariff uncertainty prompted companies to pause investment and reduce hiring, while retaliatory trade actions weakened U.S. export competitiveness. Rather than strengthening manufacturing, a reckless and unstable environment is eating away at profits and <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2025/09/29/business/trump-tariffs-small-businesses.html#:~:text=While%20many%20larger%20companies%20have,has%20been%20reducing%20other%20costs." target="_blank" >crushing small businesses.</a></p><p>The broader economic picture is equally concerning. Gross domestic product growth fell from 4.4% in the third quarter of 2025 to just 1.4% in the fourth quarter, dragged down by federal spending cuts, shutdowns and trade disruptions. For the full year, GDP <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/20/business/economy/us-economy-gdp.html" target="_blank" >grew only 2.2%</a>, down from 2.8% in 2024, representing the weakest annual expansion in nine years, excluding the pandemic. This slowdown is the result of a federal policy environment that works against economic expansion, not in service of it.</p><p>Instead of a jobs boom, the country has seen policy‑driven layoffs. Instead of massive wage growth, workers have experienced modest gains overshadowed by inflation in essential categories. Instead of a manufacturing renaissance, factory jobs have plummeted.</p><p>Campaign rhetoric is easy. Governing is harder. But when a president pledges an economic transformation and delivers chaos and catastrophe instead, voters and workers deserve to see the truth plainly: The administration’s policies have not only fallen short — they have actively undermined the economic agenda Trump promised to build.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/marc-h-morial" target="_blank" ><i>Marc H. Morial</i></a><i> is president and CEO of the National Urban League</i> <i>and</i> <i>was mayor of New Orleans from 1994 to 2002.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-1f0000" name="module-1f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Marc H. Morial

entries.title: Matisse exhibition at AIC shows a creative hitting his stride in his 70s
entries.summary: <p>Artists follow varied career paths. Some maintain strong, steady production throughout their lifetimes. Some fade over time. Some are one-hit wonders who score success with one body of work that they can never replicate.</p><p>But few accomplish what Henri Matisse did at the end of his career when his output seemingly declined. An explosion of energy and innovation re-ignited his creativity in the 1940s and thrust the septuagenarian back into the spotlight.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-620000" name="module-620000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>“Matisse’s Jazz: Rhythms in Color”</b></h3><b>When</b>: Through June 1<br><b>Where</b>: Art Institute of Chicago, 111 S. Michigan<br><b>Tickets</b>: $5 with regular museum admission<br><b>Info</b>: (312) 443-3600; <a class="Link" href="http://artic.edu" target="_blank" ><u>artic.edu</u></a></div> </div><p>The first recharged project was the 1947 artist book “Jazz,” and its featured “cut-outs” are the centerpiece of a newly opened exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago that<a class="Link" href="https://www.artic.edu/exhibitions/10557/matisse-s-jazz-rhythms-in-color" target="_blank" ><u> runs through June 1.</u></a></p><p>“Matisse’s Jazz: Rhythms in Color” is not an expansive retrospective or major survey. There is no catalog, no high-profile loans nor any big historical points being made.</p><p>That said, this compact, thoughtfully installed show has its own appeal. It offers the museum’s first-ever display of all 20 color plates from “Jazz” as well as a focused, digestible look at the artist’s trajectory to that point.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c90000" name="image-c90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2bf50f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1b16852/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20595af/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Views from the new Matisse's Jazz: Rhythms in Color exhibit at the Art Institute Chicago, in downtown Chicago, Illinois on March 9, 2026. The exhibit is composed of a series of 20 works that have not been seen together in Chicago since 1948." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20595af/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a5c3ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20595af/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F1e%2Fef59ab084888b5710ec16f63787f%2Fmatissereview-2603160119.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Matisse turned to creating cut-outs after debilitating abdominal surgery in 1941 and pursued it almost exclusively from the mid-1940s until his death in 1954.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Organized by Emily Ziemba, a research curator in the museum’s department of prints and drawings, the exhibition also gives viewers a welcome chance to see a sizable swath of the Art Institute’s expansive collection of Matisse, including pieces rarely shown. The museum acquired its first example by the artist in 1922 – a print. And it has added works by him nearly every year since, including “The Seamstress” (1900), donated by gallerist Richard Gray and his wife, Mary, in 2022.</p><p>Matisse’s cut-outs were assembled with colorful abstract and semi-abstracted shapes that he cut from gouached sheets of paper and then collaged, arranging on the walls of his studio and transferring into a host of other media. He turned to this inventive technique after debilitating abdominal surgery in 1941 and pursued it almost exclusively from the mid-1940s until his death in 1954.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c70000" name="image-c70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/17e6bab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fe0044/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f059961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Views from the new Matisse's Jazz: Rhythms in Color exhibit at the Art Institute Chicago, in downtown Chicago, Illinois on March 9, 2026. The exhibit is composed of a series of 20 works that have not been seen together in Chicago since 1948." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f059961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9fe91b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f059961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5b%2F79%2Fb8a0718e469ca3becba829a30753%2Fmatissereview-2603160012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Matisse’s cut-outs were assembled with colorful abstract and semi-abstracted shapes that he cut from gouached sheets of paper and then collaged, arranging on the walls of his studio and transferring into a host of other media</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The 20 eye-grabbing 16½-by-26-inch plates from “Jazz” were exactingly reproduced by the Tériade printing atelier in Paris using hand-painted color stencils. They depict circus scenes, mythological figures and the artist’s memories of Tahiti. Their sharp-edged forms exuberant colors and graphic boldness still throb with a sense of freshness and contemporaneity nearly 80 years after they were created.</p><p>The original edition of “Jazz” consisted of 250 copies (another 100 copies of just the color plates were also printed). The Art Institute smartly purchased its copy of the artist book a year after its release for just $375 – an unbelievable bargain from today’s vantage point.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-740000" name="image-740000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/643151a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6ddb592/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e16d735/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Views from the new Matisse's Jazz: Rhythms in Color exhibit at the Art Institute Chicago, in downtown Chicago, Illinois on March 9, 2026. The exhibit is composed of a series of 20 works that have not been seen together in Chicago since 1948. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e16d735/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5a2b91/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e16d735/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F31%2Ffc2a11404d8a90918bcbc1358357%2Fmatissereview-2603160038.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The exhibition gives viewers a welcome chance to see a sizable swath of the Art Institute’s expansive collection of Matisse, including pieces rarely shown.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In addition to “Jazz,” this show concludes with another major cut-out, “Oceania-The Sea” (1948), which textile designer Zika Ascher screenprinted and transferred onto an expansive 5½-by-12½-foot linen sheet.</p><p>Also on view are artist books and earlier artworks. The show does a nice job of highlighting some of the recurring subject matter in Matisse’s art, especially his odalisques, or reclining nudes, like the 31-inch-tall bronze “Seated Nude” (1922-29, cast 1951) or the lithograph “Large Odalisque in Striped Pantaloons” (1925).</p><p>Viewers can see hints of what was to come, none more telling than “Daisies” (1939). Sections of the oil on canvas almost appear to be cut out and collaged into place. The open, highly simplified rendering of a woman in the lower left in bold red and black prefigures his approach with the cut-outs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4d0000" name="image-4d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2f7d280/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb1f7c7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/065e169/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Views from the new Matisse's Jazz: Rhythms in Color exhibit at the Art Institute Chicago, in downtown Chicago, Illinois on March 9, 2026. The exhibit is composed of a series of 20 works that have not been seen together in Chicago since 1948." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/065e169/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aa7ee40/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/065e169/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F32%2Fb716c6df46eaac68ecd788ada1d0%2Fmatissereview-2603160104.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>In “Daisies” (1939), sections of the oil on canvas almost appear to be cut out and collaged into place.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Though it was not necessarily intended that way, “Matisse’s Jazz” serves as a perfect follow-up to a 2023-2024 show in the prints and drawings galleries that focused on Matisse’s modernist counterpart and occasional rival, Pablo Picasso.</p><p>“We haven’t done a Matisse project in our [print and drawings] galleries ever,” Ziemba said, “so I just think it was naturally time.”</p>
entries.author: Kyle MacMillan

entries.title: Older Chicagoans at high risk of losing SNAP food assistance as new work rules kick in
entries.summary: <p>Ernie Uribe has spent the past weeks hauling cans of soup and sweeping the floor of the food pantry at the First Presbyterian Church of Chicago in Woodlawn.</p><p>Uribe, 60, is trying to meet expanded work rules for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, which provides him with $252 a month to buy groceries. But the hours might not be enough. He's volunteering while spending about two nights a week helping care for his 91-year-old mother, who needs around-the-clock assistance.</p><p>“So, at the same time I’m on that ... I had to look for 80 hours,” of work or volunteering a month to meet the new rules, says Uribe, who lives in Morgan Park.</p><p>Older adults in the Chicago area are at risk of losing their SNAP benefits after work requirements were expanded to include 55- to 64-year-olds as part of President Donald Trump’s sweeping tax overhaul law passed last year. In Chicago, only about 35% of households that included adults in this age group were working at least 20 hours per week, according to a Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ analysis of data from the U.S. Census 2024 American Community Survey.</p><p>The new requirements call for people to work or volunteer 80 hours a month, which translates to about four hours a day. The rules also were expanded to include veterans and parents whose youngest child is 14 years or older. If SNAP recipients don't meet the rules or get an exemption by May, they will begin losing their benefits.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-0a0000" name="html-embed-module-0a0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/27858732"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27858732/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Older people who have been out of the workforce might find it harder to compete for jobs, especially because of continuing ageism, advocates say. This group also often spends time looking after older and younger relatives, making it harder to meet the 80 hours each month. In response, community groups across Chicago are expanding volunteer opportunities. But they aren’t seeing the expected demand and are bracing for households to be pushed out of the program.</p><p>The Illinois Department of Human Services initially estimated that as many as 400,000 people statewide could lose their benefits. By late February, IDHS dropped that to fewer than 200,000 after determining that about half of those people were eligible for an exemption, including those who are physically or mentally unable to work. That’s nearly 12% of the 1.7 million people who receive SNAP benefits in Illinois.</p><p>The potential loss of benefits comes as the number of Illinois residents 60 or older and living in poverty grew by 7% from 2023 to 2024, according to Diane Slezak, the president of AgeOptions, an advocacy group that also creates programs for elders.</p><p>“As we see this population growing, you want more healthy years,” Slezak said. “You don’t want to add sick and chronically ill years to your life.”</p><p>Republicans have said the expanded rules are meant to get more people to work and to reduce what they called abuse of the program. </p><p>But Chris Smith, who runs the food pantry for the Live Your Dreams Foundation on the Far South Side, said the elders he sees are in need and integral to helping feed families.</p><p>“You’re not going to see a 60-something-year-old person abusing SNAP benefits,” Smith said. “They literally are trying to make it stretch.”</p><p>Smith said he often hears from older people looking for specific items for a grandchild at their pantry.</p><p>“They’re still the cornerstone of our community," he said. "They really are the people that’s holding everything together."</p><h3>Push for volunteer hours </h3><p>In the basement of a Lincoln Park church, Care For Friends served a hot spaghetti lunch to more than two dozen people awaiting their chance to shop at the group’s food pantry. Hope Pavich, the group’s executive director, sees the organization as, “the last of the last of social safety nets.”</p><p>They’ve spent months trying to make sense of how the SNAP changes will affect those who come through their doors. They’ve started by creating a binder filled with volunteer opportunities within a mile of their locations. Pavich said they also are trying to think creatively of what counts as volunteering.</p><p>For example, their grocery boxes for families already include jokes for children. So Pavich thought of having a volunteer write out the jokes, which would allow someone to meet the requirement remotely.</p><p>“It moved our mission forward, and that’s something that is going to enhance our programming,” Pavich said. “So just kind of thinking outside of the box.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9a0000" name="image-9a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f727469/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7ee5a9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e12ba70/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Hope Pavich, executive director of Care For Friends, with 48th Ward Ald. Leni Manaa-Hoppenworth. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e12ba70/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8e4fde8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e12ba70/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1699x954+0+495/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2Fad%2Fa330d2e8418887ba75a27dc06469%2Fpantrycrop.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hope Pavich, executive director of Care For Friends, on the right, with 48th Ward Ald. Leni Manaa-Hoppenworth. The organization, which runs food pantries and serves hot meals, has created volunteer opportunities for SNAP recipients but Pavich worries they aren’t seeing enough demand. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Other small organizations are trying to find ways to bolster volunteer opportunities while trying to reduce the workload it could create. Nourishing Hope, which runs food pantries on the North Side and the West Side, plans to use their existing electronic records system to provide records for those who need proof of hours, according to Mitzi Baum, the group's chief executive officer.</p><p>On the South Side, another group is working with organizations in nearby Bridgeport, Brighton Park and McKinley Park to find volunteer opportunities, anticipating the challenges someone could face getting 80 hours a month at one place, said Andrea Guzmȧn, senior programs manager for Plant Chicago, which organizes markets and sells food boxes sourced from Chicago-area farmers.</p><p>And Meals on Wheels Chicago created a new program, Snap Together Volunteers, so older adults can volunteer at their monthly food pantry, which also will allow them to take home a bag of food, said Cory Morris, the organization's director of community impact.</p><p>“It’s a great way not only to get groceries and get your hours but to also socialize, just meet all these new requirements,” Morris said.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-1a0000" name="module-1a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>Help for SNAP recipients </b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-59a96830-fb08-11f0-89d5-e38f58753511"><li><b>Update your SNAP information:</b> Illinois created a <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN " target="_blank" >website</a> where you can see if you meet the new SNAP program requirements. You can update your information by calling 1-800-843-6154. <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN" target="_blank" >https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN</a></li><li><b>Find a food pantry:</b> Here’s a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/10/29/where-to-find-chicago-area-food-pantries-if-snap-goes-away" target="_blank" >list of pantries</a> in the Chicago area </li><li><b>What does the state count as an </b><a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN" target="_blank" ><b><u>exemption</u></b></a><b> from the SNAP work rules?</b> <br>Some key exemptions: a physical or mental condition that prevents you from working; pregnancy; taking part in a drug or alcohol treatment program; caregiving for another person who is incapacitated or disabled; being the main caretaker of a child under age six who lives outside your home</li><li><b>Where can I volunteer?</b> <br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-fa6424d3-1d8c-11f1-a5fc-4b8b8cb93c27"><li>The next Meals on Wheels Chicago pantry is March 24. <a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>Visit</u> this website</a> to volunteer <br><a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack</u></a> </li><li>Volunteer shifts at Nourishing Hope’s pantries are available at their <a class="Link" href="https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/" target="_blank" ><u>website</u></a><br><a class="Link" href="https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/" target="_blank" ><u>https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/</u></a></li><li>Care For Friends, 530 W. Fullerton Pkwy., can be reached at (773) 932-1010</li></ul></li></ul></div> </div><p>Each of these organizations also might be called on to prove SNAP recipients are actually volunteering. Recipients must show initial proof of hours and then again every six months. The state says they can log their hours on a “SNAP Activity Report” form or provide documentation from the organizations where they volunteer.</p><p>But fewer people so far are looking to volunteer than expected, raising concerns about what may lie ahead.</p><h3>New rules creates confusion </h3><p>Pavich worries that more people than anticipated will lose food assistance.</p><p>“I still think the people who are going to be affected still don’t know and have no idea,” Pavich said. “It’s really overwhelming the stuff that you hear all the time. It’s just easier to think, ‘That doesn’t affect me.’ And you still have the luxury of time because your SNAP card is still working.”</p><p>Meals on Wheels Chicago’s Snap Together Volunteers program also hasn’t seen strong interest, though Morris thinks that could change once more people realize they have to meet these requirements.</p><p>In anticipation of how many people might lose SNAP, groups are advocating for more state funding for programs like Meals on Wheels. The Greater Chicago Food Depository is pushing for a bill to provide onetime emergency assistance for households that lose SNAP support.</p><p>For Uribe, these new rules threaten to disrupt his newfound stability after he spent more than a year in a nursing home. A social worker helped him apply for housing and food assistance, and he is now living in Morgan Park while traveling to Hegewisch a few times a week to take care of his mother.</p><p>“That’s the one thing that I learned — you have to ask for help if you want help,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e50000" name="image-e50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/382fa83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aaf2c6c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/105c327/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/105c327/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d2f3dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/105c327/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F9a%2Ff2f57f4440da8f3cf18339a7103d%2Fsnapelders-260304-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> Ernie Uribe, 60, of Morgan Park, is among the older Chicagoans who are navigating expanded work rules to receive SNAP food assistance.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Uribe wants to comply with the new SNAP rules, but he’s finding the process confusing. He applied for an exemption due to a mental disability, but he’s unsure where that stands.<b> </b>He potentially might be exempt because of the care he provides his mother, who needs round-the-clock assistance.</p><p>“I’m really not digital-savvy,” Uribe said. “I don’t have a laptop. I do everything on my phone.”</p><p>To ensure that he keeps his benefits, his brother has been dropping him off at the food pantry at the First Presbyterian Church of Chicago to volunteer. He knows he definitely needs more hours.</p><p>Uribe and others who have applied for exemptions will not receive individual approval notices, to according the Illinois Department of Human Services, which says it would ask for additional information only “in the event that the exemption request is questionable and more information is needed to assess.”</p><p>Uribe wants lawmakers to think about stories like his when making decisions on programs like SNAP. He uses the money to buy basics like milk, cereal and eggs. He also visits a food pantry once a month.</p><p>If he loses his benefits, he said he will do what he can to get by.</p><p>“It runs out fast,” Uribe said. “I’m very fortunate, again, to receive the benefits. That’s a very big blessing that I’m getting.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Alden Loury</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-fa0000" name="module-fa0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>READ MORE</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-9bf28172-1cac-11f1-b524-93678b2f15cd"><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/26/new-snap-work-rules-leave-unemployed-englewood-man-thousands-of-others-at-risk-food-benefits-cut-off-trump" >New SNAP work rules leave unemployed Englewood man and thousands of others at risk</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/06/walmart-amazon-cps-top-employers-illinois-workers-still-need-snap-benefits-trump-work-requirements-lose-benefits" >Walmart, Amazon and CPS top list of employers where Illinois workers still need SNAP benefits</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/21/new-snap-work-requirements-cook-county-warn-health-consequences-cut-off-congress-donald-trump-food-stamps" >As new SNAP rules loom, Cook County officials warn of health consequences for people cut off</a></li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Chicago band Brigitte Calls Me Baby is back with sophomore album ‘Irreversible’
entries.summary: <p>When Brigitte Bardot died last year, it’s almost like Wes Leavins knew it was coming. The singer of Chicago indie rock band Brigitte Calls Me Baby (a name inspired by his brief pen pal relationship with the French icon) had spent months ruminating about death, existentialism and what we do with the time we have left.</p><p>“I find it to be a topic that’s on my mind a lot: loss and impermanence. There’s a fear that I have of death. And writing about it or getting close to it, without actually getting close to it, is maybe some kind of way to cope,” he said, with all of his looming thoughts influencing the material of the band’s sophomore album, “Irreversible” (out March 13 on ATO Records). Listening to dreamy retro pop songs like “The Pit,” “I Can't Have You All To Myself” and “Send Those Memories,” it’s palpable as Leavins’ powerful croon waxes about the subject with heavy introspection.</p><p>The songs were largely written on the road last year while the band (also including guitarist Jack Fluegel, bassist Devin Wessels and drummer Jeremy Benshish) was carrying on a relentless touring schedule. And after sound checks, Leavins would visit local graveyards on tour stops as a form of “exposure therapy.” Another form came in the macabre tattoos he has of an electric chair and the license plate of the Porsche that James Dean died in more than 70 years ago.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f20000" name="image-f20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jack Fluegel, Wes Leavins, Jeremy Benshish and Devin Wessels of Chicago-based band Brigitte Calls Me Baby will play a pair of shows in Chicago next month.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Scarlet Page</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I think there’s just a kind of fascination with these things for me,” he said. “But also with the tattoos, I just thought, well, I don't really want to give myself a chance to ever become like an office guy or have any other job than this,” he added.</p><p>With Brigitte Calls Me Baby’s growing profile, he hasn’t had to worry about that. Since moving to Chicago from Port Arthur, Texas in 2020 with a dream of establishing a band in the music metropolis, Brigitte Calls Me Baby has quickly ascended, capitalizing on a curious interest in their retro-rock sound that coos like the newborn baby of New Order, The Killers, The Smiths and The Cars. After a showcase at SXSW in 2023, the band was quickly signed to tastemaker label ATO Records (also home to Alabama Shakes, The Black Pumas, My Morning Jacket and Chicago artists like Friko and Neal Francis), which put out their Dave Cobb-helmed EP “This House Is Made of Corners” in 2023 followed by debut album “The Future Is Our Way Out” in 2024.</p><p>Locally, Q101 and WXRT were playing tunes like “Impressively Average” at warp speed as stations around the country like WXPN in Philadelphia and KCRW in L.A. tuned in, too. “I think that's unmistakably the moment when things changed,” said Leavins of the constant radio rotations. “That’s when people really started showing up to the gigs.” Soon after, there were invites to “Jimmy Kimmel Live!,” “CBS Saturday Morning,” Lollapalooza and All Points East as well as tour dates with Muse, Inhaler and Fontaines D.C.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" name="image-960000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28fe5b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1710x960+0+810/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1d%2F40%2F7961cc7b428f97748cc54417783f%2Fmain-band-2-23-26-alec-basse.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/05cd937/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1710x960+0+810/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1d%2F40%2F7961cc7b428f97748cc54417783f%2Fmain-band-2-23-26-alec-basse.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source alt="Brigitte Calls Me Baby" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0dd5269/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1710x960+0+810/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1d%2F40%2F7961cc7b428f97748cc54417783f%2Fmain-band-2-23-26-alec-basse.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ccfe6a3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1710x960+0+810/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1d%2F40%2F7961cc7b428f97748cc54417783f%2Fmain-band-2-23-26-alec-basse.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0dd5269/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1710x960+0+810/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1d%2F40%2F7961cc7b428f97748cc54417783f%2Fmain-band-2-23-26-alec-basse.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Even though Brigitte Calls Me Baby’s latest album seems a bit dark with its focus on death, Wes Leavins (front), said, “I think a lot of the songs, as kind of gloomy as they can seem initially, they have hope.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Alec Basse</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b></b> In that time, Leavins has also reached rock star status. Not only is he rumored to be dating Aimee Osbourne, but fans now also have tattoos — of him. “That’s been pretty mind-blowing,” he said of the permanent dedications. He’s so busy living “the life” that he doesn’t even have how own place in Chicago, opting for Airbnbs any time he’s back in town, which will be in April for two sold-out shows at Thalia Hall. “I kind of enjoy that because I love different neighborhoods,” he said. “I love Logan Square, Lincoln Square, Lincoln Park, and it gives me a chance to be in all of them.” <b></b></p><p>Much of the cross-generational interest with Brigitte Calls Me Baby has swirled around his impeccable voice and throwback look that carries touchpoints of Roy Orbison, Elvis and Morrissey (whom the band opened for in a short U.K. run last year) and has been cultivated via unique opportunities. As he was graduating high school in 2016, Leavins got the chance of a lifetime to leave Port Arthur (also the birthplace of Janis Joplin) and take a gig in a touring cast of “Million Dollar Quartet” as Elvis. Baz Luhrmann caught wind of it, and offered the singer the chance to record songs for his 2022 “Elvis” biopic, too.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/06/14/local-chicago-music-scene-rocking-again-getting-national-industry-attention" >Chicago music scene is rocking again, and it’s getting national attention</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/06/23/brigitte-calls-me-baby-review-concert-lincoln-hall-chicago-wes-leavins-set-list" >Brigitte Calls Me Baby show signals bigger gigs ahead for Chicago band</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lollapalooza/2024/08/04/lollapalooza-q-a-brigitte-calls-me-baby-wes-leavins-debut-album-the-future-is-our-way-out" >Lollapalooza Q&A: Brigitte Calls Me Baby living in ‘a whirlwind’ as new album debuts</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><b></b> “I would definitely be open to moving into some film stuff again. And certainly with the band as well, like music for film,” said Leavins. “I think that would be really rewarding and hopefully we may find our way there in the future.” <b></b></p><p>For now, the band is intent on continuing to find its groove with “Irreversible.” While songs like the first single “Slumber Party” is classic Brigitte Calls Me Baby, there’s also some more experimentation happening on “These Acts of Which We’re Designed” that offers more futuristic electro vibes.</p><p>It stemmed from sessions with a new production team this time around, with brothers Yves and Lawrence Rothman who’ve worked with Blondshell and Yves Tumors, among others. Working with the duo gave the band more “momentum,” said Leavins, adding, “There are few things more inspiring than seeing that things are actually working.” </p><p>It’s a positive spin that he hopes listeners get out of the album, too. </p><p>“I think a lot of the songs, as kind of gloomy as they can seem initially, they have hope. … It’s all about finding some kind of acceptance and a way forward.”</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Libertyville troubadour Ike Reilly is more vocal than ever
entries.summary: <p>Ike Reilly has made a career of speaking truth to power. But his blue-collar ballads, outsider anthems and punk rock poetry about the American Dream deferred have never felt more urgent than right now, a time when the Libertyville troubadour also feels more galvanized than ever.</p><p>Ahead of his live band, The Ike Reilly Assassination, taking over Schubas Tavern March 14, 15 and 17, he's been guesting at politically charged shows with his fellow L-ville pal Tom Morello.</p><p>Last November, Reilly was part of the guitarist’s “and friends” ensemble at the Vic Theater along with his son/frequent collaborator Shane and Rise Against frontman Tim McIlrath where they contributed to covers of Woody Guthrie and John Lennon. In February, a similar lineup assembled for Morello’s “resistance and solidarity” benefit concert in Minneapolis to support the families of Alex Pretti and Renee Good, who were fatally shot this year by federal immigration agents during a protest and an immigration enforcement incident, respectively.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure Reilly (left) and opening act The Neighborhood Kids. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Selena Fragassi/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/11/14/review-tom-morello-and-friends-chicago-performance-vic-theatre" >Review: Tom Morello and Friends provide the soundtrack for the resistance in powerful set at Vic Theatre</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/08/26/rise-against-chicago-punk-tour-album-ricochet-interview" >Chicago punk icons Rise Against, who built fanbase riling against authoritarianism, release new album</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2025/10/02/revolutions-goodman-theatre-tom-morello-musical-zayd-ayers-dohrn" >A rebellious Tom Morello-backed musical prepares to open as Trump’s Chicago threat escalates</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“I have so many friends up there that were suffering and depressed, and to be able to go up there and play that show … it was emotional for the whole city and for us,” Reilly said of the performance, which was held at First Avenue, a Midwest staple that he’s played nearly every year since 2001.</p><p>Emotional, too, because Reilly finally had the chance to share a stage with his longtime idol, the night’s special guest, Bruce Springsteen who had released the protest song “Streets of Minneapolis” just days prior. “I can’t even tell you how exciting it was to be on stage with my son, my pal Tom and The Boss, all sharing a microphone singing ‘Power To The People.’ And, right after, we marched,” Reilly said, hinting that there could be a chance for some kind of reunion at Springsteen’s United Center show in April.</p><p>For years, Reilly has been compared to the “Born in the U.S.A.” legend, with their catalogs sharing a strong bloodline of working-class anthems. “I had heard that he was a fan, but I never believed it,” said Reilly. That was until he took some meetings around the launch of his new SiriusXM Radio show, “Lies and Apologies” (airing the last Sunday of every month at 7 p.m. Central time on The Spectrum, in which he shares stories and spins records by bands like The Clash, Prince and The Pogues). Apparently, Springsteen had played SiriusXM CEO Scott Greenstein “Bolt Cutter,” a 2017 song Reilly wrote about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/3/16/18322138/trump-travel-ban-suffers-second-setback-in-federal-court" target="_blank" ><u>Trump’s then-travel ban</u></a>. A show offer came soon after.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b10000" name="image-b10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
