class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Martin Luther King, Jr. speaking at Quinn Chapel AME Church.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Sun-Times collection, Chicago History Museum</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At the edge of the pulpit is a lectern where many historic American figures once stood, including, the<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture/2013/08/28/long-lost-civil-rights-speech-helped-inspire-kings-dream" target="_blank" ><u> Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr</u></a>, Susan B. Anthony, Frederick Douglass, and Ida B. Wells. They denounced hate and amplified messages of freedom and equality.</p><p>Venning said the fact that Quinn Chapel still stands today is a testament to faith and the power of the divine. Quinn Chapel was built on the edge of the historic Prairie District neighborhood, an area dubbed “Millionaire's Row” where Marshall Field and George Pullman once lived.</p><p>It was an area where residents likely “didn’t want Black folks nearby,” Venning said. “We’d like to say that the Holy Spirit inspired them to leave us alone because they heard it was going to be a church, and so they didn’t bother us.”</p><p>And 133 years later, that church is still standing.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-0f0000" name="module-0f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>CHICAGO’S BLACK CHURCHES</h3><br>“Faith in Action” is an occasional series, from WBEZ and the Sun-Times, on the history and impact of the Black Church in Chicago. In addition to Quinn Chapel, the series will feature the history and work of several Black churches, including:<br><ul id="rte-4c0b4122-1e7c-11f1-a5cd-9195eabad01f"><li>Olivet Baptist Church</li><li>Vernon Park Church of God</li><li>Fellowship Missionary Baptist Church</li><li>Lawndale Christian Community Church</li><li>Progressive Baptist Church</li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: Chicago police, other agencies take in millions by seizing cars, cash even without an arrest or conviction
entries.summary: <p>Courtroom 1707 at the Daley Center. People steadily stream in, hoping they'll end up able to hold onto what's often their most valuable possession: a car.</p><p>Most are Black or Latino. Most don’t have lawyers. And many are trying to stop the government from taking their vehicles for good.</p><p>Every year, the Chicago Police Department and other law enforcement agencies across Illinois seize thousands of cars, along with piles of cash and other property. Prosecutors say the seized assets are tied to crimes that range from drug offenses to traffic violations — and occasionally violent acts.</p><p>The seizures bring in millions of dollars for law enforcement.</p><p>But critics say the Illinois civil asset forfeiture system flips on its head a foundational rule of American justice: You're innocent until proven guilty.</p><p>Under Illinois law, though, the police can seize property based on suspicion alone — even if the owner is never arrested, never charged with any crime, never convicted.</p><p>A 2023 report from the Chicago Appleseed Center for Fair Courts, an advocacy group, called for Illinois to scrap civil asset forfeitures, arguing that the system is fundamentally unfair.</p><p>U.S. Supreme Court Justice Neil Gorsuch has questioned the constitutionality of civil asset forfeiture, saying, "Even innocent owners can wait for months or years for forfeiture proceedings to play out.”</p><p>State lawmakers have passed modest reforms over the past decade, shifting the burden onto the government to prove that property seized is tied to crime and requiring agencies to provide some details on what they seize and how they spend it.</p><p>Supporters, though, say the system works and should be kept in place.</p><p>“I am 100% for this,” says Tom Weitzel, retired chief of the Riverside police department. “Activists are taking a handful of cases and exploiting them for political gain as if they represent the whole system. They don’t.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-dd0000" name="image-dd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1129c85/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f1585fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7d325a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tom Weitzel, former chief of the Riverside police department." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7d325a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a35725/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7d325a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7527x4224+0+398/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F77%2F3bc3623e4f4fac9794279640cdfe%2Fweitzel-051821-11.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tom Weitzel, former chief of the Riverside police department.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Inside ‘forfeiture court’</b></h3><p>To get a closeup look at how the system works, we went to the Daley Center, where Courtroom 1707 is Ground Zero for forfeitures in Cook County.</p><p>On Mondays and Wednesdays, dozens of people — most of them appearing on screen via Zoom — respond to lawsuits filed by prosecutors seeking to keep their property. Tuesdays and Thursdays, the trials that stem from these cases are held.</p><p>There are a lot of them. Last year, more than 3,100 forfeiture cases were filed in Cook County.</p><p>In court, the judges walk people through the maze of legal steps. You file a claim, enter an appearance, submit an answer.</p><p>For those without lawyers, though, the paperwork can be overwhelming.</p><p>Supporters of a bill being considered in Springfield say they're aiming to streamline the system by allowing prosecutors to send forfeiture notices by email instead of regular mail.</p><p>Some defense lawyers say they worry that those emailed notices could wind up buried in spam folders.</p><p>Missing one can be devastating. If people fail to respond within 45 days, a judge can order their property forfeited by default.</p><p>Even people who don’t technically own their cars — they bought them but are still paying off their car loans — can see their credit take a big hit if a vehicle is taken.</p><p>Sometimes, judges return cars temporarily when the owners argue that a seizure is causing hardship because they can’t get to work or school.</p><p>Others file what’s known as an “innocent owner” defense, claiming that someone else — often a relative — was driving their vehicle.</p><p>For many people navigating the system alone, it’s a lot to absorb and often confusing.</p><h3><b>Waiting a year for $5,000</b></h3><p>Two men appeared in court recently trying to recover about $5,000 each that Chicago police officers took from them after a traffic stop in which officers found a small amount of cannabis. The men say they’re auto dealers and were carrying the cash to buy a vehicle. They told a judge the misdemeanor drug charges against them were dismissed.</p><p>But their forfeiture case is still dragging on.</p><p>In early March, the officer involved failed to appear in court. That prompted a delay until late April.</p><p>The men — who said they couldn’t afford a lawyer because his fee would be more than what they're trying to get back — have now been waiting nearly a year.</p><p>“This thing just keeps dragging on with no end in sight,” says one, speaking on the condition of not being named.</p><h3><b>One man’s win</b></h3><p>Every once in a while, someone scores a victory.</p><p>Wynton Kyle Dozier, 40, got his 2014 Ford Fusion back on March 5 when prosecutors dropped his forfeiture case.</p><p>Chicago police officers had arrested Dozier the day after Christmas in 2024 after stopping him for failing to use a turn signal. Officers said they found drugs in the car.</p><p>In August, a judge threw out the criminal case, ruling the search was illegal.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5b0000" name="image-5b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a06b7b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee3c224/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7490195/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A 2010 Ford Fusion Hybrid." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7490195/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d4c7810/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7490195/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x1122+0+11/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Faa%2Faf7c7ff740ce8a79eb739d093c29%2F2-9-cea4-fusion10-p177821.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A 2010 Ford Fusion Hybrid.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times file.</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Dozier hired a lawyer to fight the criminal charges, but he handled the forfeiture case on his own. It took more than eight months to recover his car, which prosecutors valued at $4,150.</p><p>“They just want you to give up and take your things for free,” Dozier says.</p><p>He says he never understood why he had to fight two legal battles — one for his freedom and another for his car.</p><p>“It doesn’t make sense,” he says.</p><p>Another man, Maurice Walker, 63, decided to make a deal. His 2013 Cadillac, valued at $2,800, was seized in Markham after police in the suburb stopped him last April for running a red light. He was arrested for driving on a license suspended because of a DUI, a charge prosecutors dropped last July<b>.</b></p><p>Complicating his case, prosecutors had released his Cadillac CTS to a woman who said she needed the car, claiming a hardship.</p><p>At a recent forfeiture hearing, Walker was confused why he still needed to fight to get his car back.</p><p>“My case was dismissed,” he told the judge. “How do I owe you anything? The only thing left is them trying to take my car.”</p><p>A prosecutor responded: “The criminal case don’t mean nothing.”</p><p>The judge explained the legal differences between a criminal case and a civil forfeiture case.</p><p>After huddling with the prosecutor, Walker agreed to give the police $1,050 for fees for towing and storing his car.</p><p>The judge told Walker he wouldn’t have to go to trial to fight for his car as long as he pays up by May.</p><p>“I’m OK with that,” Walker replied. “Thank you, judge.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/02/20/dea-cash-forfeitures-trump-biden-gold-miner-civil-rights " target="_blank" >DEA didn’t arrest gold miner stopped at Union Station but seized his $115K and kept it for 7 years</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h3><b>Millions to Chicago police</b></h3><p>How often property owners win or lose isn't clear. Those numbers aren't reported to the Illinois State Police.</p><p>The money involved is easier to trace. Under state law, proceeds from seized cash and auctioned property are distributed by the state police. The seizing agency gets 65%, the local state’s attorney’s office 12.5%, appellate prosecutors 12.5%, and the state police 10% for administering the program.</p><p>According to the latest report from the state police, reflecting 2024 seizures, the Chicago Police Department took in $9.4 million in assets that year — including 962 vehicles worth more than $7.7 million, plus cash. Every other law enforcement agency in Illinois combined reported seizing 595 vehicles.</p><p>A Chicago police spokesman says civil asset forfeiture "plays an important role in disrupting organized criminal networks," saying the money goes toward “strengthening public safety."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-570000" name="module-570000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>CHICAGO POLICE FORFEITURES</h3><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-78d3ea20-1e10-11f1-a08f-7df7c31be9ee" data-pm-slice="3 3 []"><li>In 2024, the Chicago Police Department collected $1.3 million from the Illinois State Police through the process of various agencies sharing the money from civil asset forfeitures.</li><li>The department spent $1.4 million, including $1.2 million for operating expenses, $77,000 for informants and undercover drug buys and $67,000 for travel, meals and training. </li><li>In recent years, the department also spent forfeiture funds on car leases, phone service, vehicle repairs, lab equipment — and hundreds of thousands of dollars that went to a company that supplies surveillance technology to law enforcement agencies, including U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, records show.</li><li>Those records also show that, in April 2025, roughly $4.5 million was sitting in the Chicago Police Department’s drug forfeiture fund along with $409,000 in a forfeiture fund tied to money-laundering seizures. That was nearly 40% less than the department held in 2020 — a drop sources say is tied to a sharp decline in search warrants following reforms prompted by wrongful police raids.</li></ul></div> </div><p>Under a law that took effect Jan. 1, the state police will collect even more information from police agencies, including the property owner's race, sex, age and ZIP code as well as more data about the spending of forfeiture money, including purchases of surveillance technology.</p><h3>A tool — or abuse?</h3><p>Despite the big money involved, Weitzel, the former Riverside police chief, rejects the idea that civil asset forfeiture is a money grab.</p><p>Forfeiture is “one of the few tools that weakens criminal networks financially, not just through arrests,” Weitzel says.</p><p>He says strict controls governed how his department seized and spent forfeiture funds.</p><p>But critics say the system needs changes. Some point to Michigan, where Gov. Gretchen Whitmer signed bipartisan legislation in 2019 largely banning police from forfeiting property worth $50,000 or less without a criminal conviction. Lawmakers later modified the law to allow forfeitures over $20,000 at airports without a conviction.</p><p>If that law applied in Illinois, lots of the civil asset forfeiture cases that now go through Courtroom 1707 would never be filed.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-660000" name="image-660000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/46f5e6e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F5e%2F40bbd3984df3ac75f5632fecaf86%2Fdeacashgrab-2602220047.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/82b3671/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F5e%2F40bbd3984df3ac75f5632fecaf86%2Fdeacashgrab-2602220047.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Greta Keranen and her husband Jeff Regnier with two of the Ford Broncos they recovered from the government after Will County prosecutors seized them in a civil asset forfeiture case.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><h3>Ex-gun store owner, wife say Will County prosecutors wrongfully seized cars, cash in moves judge called ‘authoritarian’</h3><br>On a frigid morning in February 2023, officers smashed through the door of a sprawling Will County home, tossing flash-bang grenades as they stormed inside.<br><br>Their targets were Jeff Regnier and Greta Keranen, a couple accused of pocketing $275,000 through federal <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/ppp-paycheck-protection-program-covid-relief" target="_blank" >COVID-19 relief programs.</a> Officers also raided the home of Regnier’s father — a retired Chicago cop — though he never was charged with any crime.<br><br>The criminal case against Regnier and Keranen is still in court. But, in a dramatic twist, the couple has beaten a separate attempt by Will County prosecutors to seize millions of dollars in investment accounts and six vehicles under Illinois’ civil asset forfeiture laws.<br><br>Two of those vehicles — late-model Ford Broncos — prompted a blistering rebuke to prosecutors from a Will County judge who ordered the SUVs returned in January.<br><br>“The money-laundering statute is not a catchall for all things that the state cannot find in a legitimate way to seize,” Judge Brian Barrett wrote.<br><br>Barrett said he was “particularly disturbed with the special authoritarianism” displayed by the government.<br><br>Will County prosecutors are appealing.<br><br>The Regnier-Keranen saga spotlights the hotly debated civil asset forfeiture system, which allows police and prosecutors to seize property they believe is tied to crime — even if the owner is never convicted of any crime.<br><br>Civil rights advocates say the system flips the presumption of innocence on its head.<br><br>Not so, according to the Will County state’s attorney’s office, which says its prosecutors use “all legally available channels to fight crime and serve the residents of Will County,” noting that the Illinois General Assembly has deemed forfeiture “an important tool” and made reforms to the system in 2018.<br><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> He opened Kee Firearms & Training in New Lenox in 2020 as the pandemic swept the country. Village officials initially resisted the business, but the federally licensed gun dealer pressed ahead.<br><br>At its peak, Regnier said, the shop employed about 60 people.<br><br><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/2/5/22269259/illinois-gun-sales-january-capitol-seige-insurrection-pandemic-protests-firerarms" target="_blank" >Gun sales</a> generated so much cash that Regnier hired a Brink’s armored truck to haul deposits to the bank.<br><br>Those deposits triggered alarms. The bank flagged the transactions to federal authorities as potentially suspicious. The Secret Service investigated but never filed federal charges. Frank Andreano, the couple’s lawyer, said a federal audit of the store found no wrongdoing.<br><br>But, in February 2023, Will County prosecutors charged the couple with fraud tied to pandemic relief programs, including the Paycheck Protection Program created to help businesses survive COVID-19 restrictions.<br><br>Prosecutors accused the couple of falsifying loan applications with inflated revenue figures for the gun shop and a construction company owned by Keranen.<br><br>A month later, prosecutors launched a parallel civil forfeiture case seeking millions of dollars from the couple’s Fidelity retirement accounts and four vehicles — an Audi sedan, a Ford F-250 pickup and two Ford Broncos.<br><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> A judge said “authoritarianism” was on display by the government.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><h3><b>A second clash over Broncos</b></h3><br>The dispute escalated in April 2023, when Keranen returned to a dealership where she previously financed vehicles and took out a loan to buy two more Ford Broncos to replace the ones seized.<br><br>Prosecutors responded by filing another criminal case, accusing the couple of fraud and saying Keranen inflated the revenue of her construction company on the loan application.<br><br>Authorities also filed a second forfeiture case to seize the newly purchased Broncos. But those efforts collapsed.<br><br>Will County judges acquitted the couple of the criminal charges tied to the April 2023 vehicle purchase and dismissed the forfeiture lawsuits seeking their retirement accounts and six vehicles.<br><br>But prosecutors are appealing Barrett’s order returning the Broncos bought in April 2023.<br><br>In an appeal of the civil ruling, the state’s attorney’s office argues that the judge ignored evidence, including bank records showing Keranen claimed $400,000 in monthly gross profits on the loan application. Under the law, prosecutors can’t appeal the acquittal in the criminal case.<br><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7d0000" name="image-7d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b499c26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/05039b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/076b4a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jeff Regnier, owner of now-closed Kee Firearms & Training, stands next to a display case in his New Lenox store in 2021. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/076b4a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a5e6de6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/076b4a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5463x3066+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fce%2Fa033aad94e37bdfadcdad57a5a95%2Fguns-020121-16.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jeff Regnier, owner of now-closed Kee Firearms & Training, at his New Lenox store in 2021. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div>Regnier said the legal battle prompted him to close his gun store and transfer his firearms inventory to his sister in Georgia, a sheriff’s deputy who is also a federally licensed firearms dealer.<br><br>“We still owe the IRS money,” Regnier said. “Mortgages. Attorneys. Workers. It’s been tough.”<br><br>“They have made life very difficult for us,” Keranen said.<br><br>The couple eventually recovered $4.4 million from frozen investment accounts but say Will County still owes them about $2 million in lost investment gains.<br><br><h3>More legal fights ahead</h3><br>The original criminal case tied to the pandemic loans is now on hold as a judge considers a request from the couple to appoint a special prosecutor to investigate Will County’s forfeiture unit and federal agents.<br><br>In a court filing, the couple’s lawyer argues that the alleged abuses of the forfeiture system in the case were “neither isolated nor an aberration in Will County.”<br><br>Andreano said forfeiture laws allow government agencies to build “slush funds” with little oversight.<br><br>He also said prosecutors had a financial incentive in the couple’s case: Any restitution ordered in the criminal case would go to the federal Small Business Administration, not to Will County.<br><br>“So they filed a forfeiture case to grab money for the county,” he said.<br><br>Andreano is challenging Illinois forfeiture laws in another case, this one involving an 85-year-old woman whose 2014 Mazda CX-5 was seized by New Lenox police after her daughter was stopped for an expired registration sticker.<br><br>Regnier, who ran unsuccessfully for Congress in a Republican primary in 2022, said the forfeiture system gives prosecutors enormous leverage.<br><br>“They hold you under water and say, ‘Let’s make a deal,’ ” he said.<br><br>Keranen said she witnessed the human toll of the system while in court.<br><br>“We saw people coming into the forfeiture court crying,” she said. “I think it needs reform. I’m not sure it needs to be abolished. But they shouldn’t be able to take your stuff without proving a crime.”</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Frank Main

entries.title: Big tech companies sue to block Chicago's new social media tax
entries.summary: <p>A coalition of the world’s largest tech companies is suing to block Chicago’s new tax on social media giants like TikTok, Facebook and X, claiming the city’s first-of-its-kind, per-user levy tramples over the First Amendment rights of major corporations that contend they’re part of the free press.</p><p>The lawsuit filed Friday in Cook County Circuit Court by the tech trade group NetChoice takes aim at the social media tax <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/10/21/brandon-johnson-social-media-tax-chicago-budget" target="_blank" ><u>proposed by Mayor Brandon Johnson</u></a> and retained in the budget ultimately approved by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/12/20/chicago-budget-veto-brandon-johnson-alternate-head-tax" target="_blank" ><u>a recalcitrant City Council</u></a> last fall.</p><p>Companies started paying the tax last month at a rate of $0.50 per user after the first 100,000 Chicagoans who log onto Snapchat, Reddit, Instagram and other popular sites, with city officials projecting a $31 million annual windfall for future mental health programs.</p><p>Big Tech wants to nip the nation’s first-ever enacted social media tax in the bud as several states, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/springfield/2026/02/18/jb-pritzker-budget-proposal-state-of-state-social-media-tax" target="_blank" ><u>including Illinois</u></a>, consider similar measures to wring money from companies to offset the public health threat many experts say their platforms can pose, especially for young people.</p><p>But social media companies have long argued they shouldn’t bear all the blame for a youth mental health crisis, and in their suit, they say they can’t be targeted for taxation any more than a newspaper can.</p><p>“They are singling out certain publishers for disfavored taxation for having a broad audience, and the Supreme Court has rejected that,” said Paul Taske, co-director of the NetChoice Litigation Center. “This tax and taxes like it are essentially a penalty on publishing popular speech. The city of Chicago is attempting to penalize popular services for the fact that they’re reaching broad audiences, and that is unconstitutional.”</p><p>Experts previously told the Sun-Times the city tax was on shaky legal ground, largely based on a 1983 U.S. Supreme Court decision that blocked a Minnesota state tax on newspapers for paper and ink purchases beyond a $100,000 yearly threshold. Former Justice Sandra Day O’Connor wrote for the court that the tax “singled out the press for special treatment” without justification. </p><p>Such precedent is "not limited to the journalistic press," according to the NetChoice suit, which cites another Supreme Court ruling that First Amendment protections extend to "every sort of publication which affords a vehicle of information and opinion."</p><p>Lawmakers in other states considering similar legislation have likened taxing companies for the data they collect from social media users to taxing mining companies for the minerals they extract from the earth. Taske says that’s “not a real response to the problems that these taxes pose. Fundamentally, they’re taxes on speech.”</p><p>Johnson has argued that social media companies that have been allowed to “collect our data and sell it for profit” have implemented “more and more aggressive strategies” and algorithms to get people “addicted” to social media. In 2024, former U.S. Surgeon General Vivek Murthy proposed warning labels for social media sites like those on packs of cigarettes.</p><p>The mayor’s office has categorized it as an amusement tax, which NetChoice rejects because it’s “not calculated based on consumers’ use of social media.” Their suit contends it’s based on the data the social companies collect from users and monetize just like “myriad other online services collect data from their users — and those services are left untaxed.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/12/30/chicago-sports-betting-tax-lawsuit-tro-draftkings-fanduel" >FanDuel, DraftKings, other big sportsbooks sue to block new Chicago sports betting tax</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Gov. JB Pritzker included a similar tax in his state budget proposal last month to push back against social media sites that “profit off of surveilling youth, creating addictive algorithms, and abandoning their responsibility to keep kids and consumers safe,” according to his office.</p><p>Pritzker’s plan — which the Illinois General Assembly will consider over the next two months before passing a budget by the end of May — would charge the companies $0.10 per user from 100,000 up to 500,000 users; $40,000 plus $0.25 per user up to a million; and $165,000 plus $0.50 per user over a million. If approved, it’s expected to generate some $200 million for the state.</p><p>The governor's office has insisted on calling it a “platform fee” rather than a tax, arguing the distinction is that payments would be based on a flat number of users rather than directly tied to company revenue.</p><p>Taske said that “obfuscates the fact that it would operate like a tax: trying to collect money for a particular thing,” in this case an audience consuming speech online.</p><p>“This should make them think twice about proceeding,” Taske said.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Woman gunned down in West Side alley
entries.summary: <p>Police are trying to find out who fatally shot a woman found dead in a Garfield Park neighborhood alley Friday morning.</p><p>The victim, a woman of an unknown age, suffered one gunshot wound to the left flank area, in the 200 block of North Kilbourn Avenue, according to police. </p><p>She was dead on the scene, according to police. </p><p>Area 4 detectives are investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: New breed of expansion teams in Toronto and Portland to challenge the Sky in 2026
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> will face two new opponents this season: the Portland Fire and the Toronto Tempo. So how seriously should they take the newcomers from up north?</p><p>Traditionally, expansion teams were the league’s punching bags, a rite of passage the Sky know well. In their 2006 debut, they won just five games. Six losing seasons followed before the Sky finally reached the playoffs.</p><p>It’s not just the Sky — building a roster from scratch isn’t easy. You draft players from other teams’ benches. You don’t get a lottery pick in the college draft.</p><p>In many ways, expansion teams are built to suffer.</p><p>Then the Valkyries shattered that expectation.</p><p>They became the winningest expansion team in league history last season, making the playoffs and sweeping the Sky 3-0.</p><p>They had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/06/27/tyler-marsh-and-natalie-nakase-face-off-as-head-coaches-for-the-first-time" >everything going for them</a>. Deep-pocketed ownership. A one-of-a-kind fan atmosphere. A roster built from international gems and an elite coach.</p><p>The looming question for the Sky is whether the Valkyries are the start of a new trend. Because the league is going to keep expanding: three more teams will join starting in 2028.</p><p><b>Brondello is the Tempo’s ace</b></p><p>The Tempo might have the best shot at replicating the Valkyries’ blueprint. They have an ace in head coach Sandy Brondello, a two-time champion with strong relationships across the league’s superstar ranks. The Tempo smartly made her one of only a handful of coaches earning more than $1 million.</p><p>The Tempo front office also signaled they’re on top of the latest trend: building rosters around data, analytics and high-level scouting. General manager Monica Wright Rogers and assistant general manager Eli Horowitz bring experience with this from the Mercury and Sparks, respectively.</p><p>They’ll also have a nation cheering them on. The Tempo sold out season tickets in Toronto and will play games in Montreal and Vancouver, establishing themselves as Canada’s team.</p><p><b>Fire are poaching from the best</b></p><p>Portland’s path looks more experimental. Their coach, 30-year-old Alex Sarama, was an NBA assistant and lacks Brondello’s league relationships and institutional knowledge.</p><p>But the organization has been poaching from the right places. Before joining the Fire, general manager Vanja Černivec helped build the Valkyries roster. Her ambition is to build the best analytics department in the league, and she already plucked an analyst from the 2024 champion Liberty.</p><p>The Fire may have an edge over the Tempo <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/13/in-the-wnba-facilities-race-sky-hold-a-momentary-advantage" >in facilities</a>. They'll play in the Moda Center, an arena roughly twice the size of the Tempo’s, and will practice in a brand-new facility. The Tempo will practice at the University of Toronto this season.</p><p><b>Sky on track?</b></p><p>While the Fire and Tempo have plenty to prove, they’re clearly a different breed of expansion team than the Sky were. Twenty years ago, Sky investors <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/19/from-passion-to-profit-can-the-skys-founding-mission-survive-the-leagues-success" >saw the team more as a civic and social justice project</a> than a major commercial venture. Now, they must figure out how to compete with expansion groups that can commit more than $100 million as an opening gambit.</p><p>The Sky’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/06/skys-new-practice-facility-will-be-ready-by-late-spring-ceo-adam-fox-says" >new practice facility in Bedford Park</a> is part of their attempt to keep pace. The team says the project cost has risen to $60 million, though Bedford Park is covering roughly half.</p><p>This season should also reveal the Sky’s progress in the two other key areas: coaching and international scouting. Tyler Marsh enters his second season as head coach, and general manager Jeff Pagliocca’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/11/28/setting-expectations-for-20-year-old-sky-prospect-ajsa-sivka" >first international prospect</a>, Ajša Sivka, is expected to join the roster.</p><p>Where the Sky have not yet made moves is the basketball strategy department. While the Tempo and Fire have both prioritized dedicated hires in analytics and scouting, the Sky front office remains lean. They lack support roles such as an assistant GM or data analysts, leaving the bulk of the strategic lift to the coaching staff.</p><p>If Toronto or Portland prove that the Valkyries were no fluke, the Sky may need to rethink its intellectual infrastructure.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Winds nearing 60 mph knock down trees, power lines and spark 'very difficult' driving in Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>Extremely windy weather persists in the Chicago area, with gusts nearing 60 mph, strong enough to knock down tree limbs, make driving very difficult and lead to some power outages, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>A high wind warning was in effect for the Chicago area until 4 p.m. as forecasters anticipate gusts will continue surpassing 60 mph.</p><p>ComEd is reporting nearly 20,000 outages, largely centered on the North Side and in the northwest suburbs as of about 8:08 a.m., according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.comed.com/outages/experiencing-an-outage/outage-map" target="_blank" >their outage map.</a></p><p>A spokesperson for ComEd told the Sun-Times that emergency crews who helped restore power to customers during severe storms earlier this week were already in the area and providing assistance. So far, power has been restored to 47,000 customers Friday, according to ComEd. </p><p>"We're going to be out in full force getting people restored," the spokesperson said. </p><p>ComEd noted customers are being impacted "for a number of reasons" related to the wind, from electrical poles being knocked down to tree branches blowing into power lines. </p><p>There's no immediate timeline for when all customers will have their power restored. </p><p>"It's not like a thunderstorm where it passes by, these are ongoing winds through the day," the spokesperson said. "We're just going to keep banging away and getting people restored as quickly as we can." </p><p>The wind caused a brief ground stop at O'Hare International Airport, which expired about 7:15 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. </p><p>Train service was also impacted by the wind. Metra Union Pacific train #312 scheduled to depart from Highland Park was running 20 minutes behind schedule due to an obstruction on the tracks. On the CTA Yellow Line, service was temporarily suspended for the same reason. </p><p>Wind gusts at O'Hare and Midway Airports peaked at 53 and 56 mph respectively as of about 5 a.m., the National Weather Service said. Valparaiso Airport in Indiana reported gusts at 63 mph and DuPage Airport reached 62 mph. </p><p>"It's sort of the same as a severe thunderstorm," said Brett Borchardt, a meteorologist with the weather service. "It's a wind strong enough to knock down tree limbs, make driving very difficult and even lead to some power outages."</p><p>The strong winds originated from a low pressure system moving toward Lake Michigan from Madison, Wisconsin, where northern Illinois and northwest Indiana are on the southern end of the system. </p><p>"It's just the strength of this low pressure system that is driving these winds to be so strong," Borchardt said. </p><p>Borchardt notes that Saturday looks to be a "break in the action" before another storm system approaches Sunday night. </p><p>The system could potentially bring severe thunderstorms and snowfall and temperatures are expected to plummet from the 40s to the 20s Monday and Tuesday, according to the weather service. The wind chill could struggle to get above minus five degrees by early next week. </p><p>"That's gonna be a storm system that could give us a little bit of everything," Borchardt said. </p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Blackhawks to play two games in Dusseldorf next season as NHL invests in Germany
entries.summary: <p>The NHL wisely plans to focus its European strategy on Germany in the coming years, and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> will be part of their first injection of new investment.</p><p>The Hawks will play two games against the Senators at the PSD Bank Dome in Dusseldorf, Germany, on Dec. 18-20 next season. Senators forward Tim Stutzle, already one of the best German NHL players of all time, will be a big draw.</p><p>It will mark the Hawks' first trip to Europe since they visited Berlin and Prague in fall 2019.</p><p>It will also mark the second and third NHL regular-season games ever played in Germany — and the first in 15 years. The league plans to play games in Germany in 2027-28 and 2028-29, as well.</p><p>"Our deepened commitment to Germany as an important hockey country marks a critical next chapter for the NHL," deputy commissioner Bill Daly said in a statement.</p><p>"By further investing in one of Europe’s most dynamic sports markets — both through the execution of major events and the development and implementation of grassroots initiatives — we intend to strengthen our presence and build stronger connections with German fans, partners and local communities. In prioritizing our efforts here, we hope to accelerate hockey’s growth across the region, creating meaningful impact from the ground up."</p><p><i>This story will be updated.</i></p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Man stabbed on Red Line platform in River North
entries.summary: <p>A 37-year-old man was stabbed with a sharp object while at a Red Line subway station in River North late Thursday.</p><p>The victim was standing on the platform, 521 N. State St., when an man he doesn't know approached, displayed a sharp object and attacked him shortly before midnight, police said. </p><p>The man, who suffered a puncture wound to the left tricep, was taken in fair condition to Northwestern Memorial Hospital, police said. </p><p>It wasn't clear if the attacker was arrested. Area 3 detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Southwest Airlines dropping service at O'Hare
entries.summary: <p>Southwest Airlines plans to depart from O'Hare International Airport for good in June, ending a five-year run at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/01/20/ohare-reclaims-top-spot-named-nations-busiest-airport-in-2025" target="_blank" >nation’s busiest airport</a>.</p><p>The airline made the announcement in emails to passengers Friday morning, saying that all of its flights at O'Hare would be canceled starting June 4. Passengers were given rebooking and refund options.</p><p>Southwest said it will continue operations at Midway Airport, its regional hub.</p><p>The airline's move comes as the Federal Aviation Administration pushes carriers to reduce flights at O'Hare this summer to reduce what it has called over-scheduling. United and American airlines have been engaged in a battle for gates at O'Hare, and have been adding flights.</p><p>In a statement to the media, the Southwest said it was discontinuing O'Hare service as part of its "ongoing efforts to refine its network."</p><p>"Operating at Chicago O’Hare continues to be challenging, and we are confident we can serve Chicagoland through Chicago Midway," the airline said in a statement.</p><p>Southwest <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2021/2/14/22282861/southwest-airlines-service-ohare-midway" target="_blank" >began service at O’Hare</a> in February 2021, offering 20 daily departures. At the time, the airline said the move gave flexibility and convenience to its North Side customers.</p><p>Southwest's statement on Friday did not address the FAA's threat to cut flights at O'Hare this summer. </p><p>But that was likely an issue the company was considering, said Joe Schwieterman, director of DePaul University's Chaddick Institute for Metropolitan Development.</p><p>"The prospect of having to cut flights due to FAA mandates creates real difficulties if they stay at O'Hare. The FAA may impose across-the-board cuts," Schwieterman said.</p><p>Also, Southwest may have considered its competition with the airport's larger carriers, United and American, which have been adding flights.</p><p>"It is harder to be a minor player at the airport with intense competition nearby," he said.</p><p>Losing Southwest also compounds a gate imbalance at O'Hare, according to Schwieterman.</p><p>Southwest operates out of Terminal 5, the Global Terminal that is physically separated from Terminals 1, 2 and 3, where United and American mostly operate.</p><p>"There's plenty of gate capacity in Terminal 5. But [Terminal] 1 to 3 are clogged with traffic," he said. "This adds to that imbalance. United and American don't want to move flights out there."</p><p>The Chicago Department of Aviation, which runs O'Hare and Midway, said in a statement that it understands Southwest's "need to continually evaluate its network and make the appropriate business decisions." The Department said it "looks forward to welcoming Southwest back to O'Hare International Airport in the future."</p><p>Southwest said affected employees at O'Hare can apply for positions across its network, including at Midway, where the airline maintains its hub.</p><p>Southwest has served Midway on the Southwest Side for 41 years, and operates 244 daily departures to 80 destinations. The airline said its Midway service already flies to the 15 destinations currently served from O'Hare.</p><p>The airline also said it is ending service to Washington Dulles International Airport.</p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Analyzing the first week of Bears free agency
entries.summary: <p>At the end of the first week of free agency, the Sun-Times’ Patrick Finley and Jason Lieser break down the Bears’ decisions — and moves:</p><h3><b>What message did Ryan Poles and Ben Johnson send by staying conservative in free agency?</b></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: That the fastest path for the team to go from good to great is for quarterback Caleb Williams to make the leap next year that Drake Maye did last season.<br>On paper, the Bears are no better today than they were on March 1. The major gains, then, have to come from within — from Williams and receiver Rome Odunze in Year 3, and tight end Colston Loveland and receiver Luther Burden in Year 2.</p><p><b>LIESER:</b> They’re confident in what they have, both on the roster and on their coaching staff. Clearly the Bears believe they can solve their issues on the defensive and offensive lines and in the secondary between talent in the building and incoming draft picks.<br>At left tackle, for example, they seem content to patch it together with Braxton Jones, Theo Benedet, Kiran Amegadjie and perhaps a rookie. Maybe they can help former first-rounder Jedrick Wills get back on track. But the overarching idea is that Johnson can figure it out.<br> </p><h3><b>What was the Bears’ best move? </b></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: It would have been easy to panic when center Drew Dalman retired. General manager Ryan Poles' decision to trade a 2027 fifth-round pick for starting center Garrett Bradbury, though, was a tidy piece of business. Free agent Tyler Linderbaum became the highest-paid center ever — the Raiders gave him $81 million over three years — and Connor McGovern got $52 million over four years to stay in Buffalo. Bradbury, who helped develop Maye and is college buddies with Joe Thuney, only takes a $5.7 million cap hit next year.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: There's no doubt the Bears will miss safety Kevin Byard on and off the field, but they opted to reset at the position by signing former Seahawks standout Coby Bryant rather than bank on Byard continuing to star as he turns 33 before the season. The upside on Bryant, who signed for a modest $40 million over three years, is huge.<br> </p><h3><b>What was the Bears’ worst move? </b></h3><h3></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: Safeties Jaquan Brisker and Byard and cornerback Nashshon Wright all played more than 97% of the team’s snaps last year — and now all three are gone. The Bears wanted to get faster, but I’d rather give Wright $5.5 million, as the Jets did, than bank on Tyrique Stevenson as a starter. </p><p><b>LIESER</b>: Not making one at defensive end. No Maxx Crosby. No Trey Hendrickson. No real changes to a pass rush that has been second-worst in sacks over the last four seasons.<br> </p><h3><b>Which player will they miss the most?</b></h3><h3></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: Dalman, who posted a Pro Bowl season last year and appeared to be the long-term answer to a long-time problem. The Bears had seven different Week 1 centers from 2015-25.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: Losing receiver DJ Moore is going to hurt more than anyone seems to realize inside or outside Halas Hall. His numbers fell to career lows last season, but he’s incredibly smart and versatile and could’ve been an ideal chess piece for Johnson.<br> </p><h3><b>What is the biggest remaining need after the first wave of free agency?</b></h3><h3></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: An edge rusher. And they won’t be able to find a relevant one until the draft.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: The thought of going into another season without left tackle solidified is troubling. The Bears had a four-way battle deep into camp last year and benched two different starters before committing to Ozzy Trapilo, who now is out long-term with a knee injury.<br> </p><h3><b>Who should they be targeting in the draft? </b></h3><p></p><p><b>FINLEY</b>: The best edge rusher available at No. 25, be it Clemson’s T.J. Parker, Missouri’s Zion Young, Auburn’s Keldric Faulk or Miami’s Ahkeem Mesidor.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: Clemson offensive tackle Monroe Freeling in Round 1. The best move with Trapilo out is to look for lasting solution rather than a stopgap. There’s no certainty about how Trapilo will play post-injury, and if the Bears end up with a surplus at the position, they’ll have valuable trade capital.</p><h3> </h3><h3><b>Who at Halas Hall is under the most pressure now?</b></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>:<b> </b>Defensive coordinator Dennis Allen. Take away the takeaways, and the Bears defense stunk last year. They gave up the fourth-most yards and 10<sup>th</sup>-most points. Credit the Bears for recognizing that and turning over both starting safeties, one cornerback and two linebackers. The Bears focused on bringing in fast, versatile players to take their place because it fit best Allen’s system. Now it’s on him to make it work.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: Defensive end Montez Sweat. Sweat has been good, not great, and the Bears haven’t gotten what they paid for. They gave up a second-round pick to get him and signed him to a four-year, $98 million deal. He has the 13th-highest salary-cap hit in the NFL this season and needs to play at that level or better.</p><h3> </h3><h3><b>What needs to happen for the Bears to repeat as NFC North champs?</b></h3><p><b>FINLEY</b>: Regression around the division. Quarterback Kyler Murray can’t turn into a star in Minnesota. The Lions’ third offensive coordinator in three years needs to be as ineffective as the second one was. And the Packers need to look a lot more like they did in the second half last year than the first.</p><p><b>LIESER</b>: Quite a bit, but the starting point is their young players breaking out. Williams, Odunze and Loveland all need to be stars.<br></p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Fiscalía retira los cargos de conspiración contra 2 manifestantes de ICE
entries.summary: <p>El jueves, los fiscales federales desestimaron los cargos de conspiración que habían presentado contra dos de las seis personas que protestaron el otoño pasado frente a la instalación de detención de inmigrantes en los suburbios, incluyendo a una ex candidata a la Junta del Condado de Cook que suspendió su campaña para las elecciones primarias del martes para luchar contra los cargos.</p><p>El grupo de acusados, conocido como los “Broadview Six”, quedará reducido a cuatro si la jueza del distrito April Perry concede la moción presentada por los fiscales federales. Esta busca desestimar los cargos contra <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/06/cook-county-board-candidate-suspends-campaign-to-fight-indictment" target="_blank" >Catherine “Cat” Sharp</a> y la artista Joselyn Walsh.</p><p>Sharp expresó que la decisión "prueba lo que siempre hemos sabido, que la acusación en este caso fue defectuosa desde el principio.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5f0000" name="module-5f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"Aunque estoy, por supuesto, emocionada por este resultado, el abuso del [Servicio de Inmigración y Aduanas (ICE)] contra inmigrantes y refugiados en nuestras comunidades continúa", dijo.</p><p>Walsh comentó que la desestimación de la acusación "no cambia la interrupción que causó en mi vida durante los últimos seis meses”.</p><p>"También no cambia que fui víctima de la violencia de ICE cuando le dispararon a mi guitarra y que muchos siguen experimentando violencia a manos de agentes federales", añadió.</p><p>El fiscal adjunto William Hogan escribió en la moción del jueves que, "como hace la oficina del fiscal en cada caso, el gobierno ha seguido evaluando nuevos hechos, pruebas e información para asegurarse de que se sirvan los intereses de la justicia”.</p><p>“Por lo tanto, el Gobierno de los Estados Unidos solicita desestimar con prejuicio la acusación contra Catherine Sharp y Joselyn Walsh, en interés de la justicia”, añadió.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9f6b04" name="image-9f6b04"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc5540d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/63334f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc5540d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Joselyn Walsh toca la guitarra en la Federal Plaza después de su presentación ante el juez por un cargo de conspiración federal.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/Para el Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Aún enfrentan cargos la candidata al Congreso, Kat Abughazaleh, el concejal de Oak Park Brian Straw, el miembro del comité demócrata del 45° distrito Michael Rabbitt y Andre Martin. Se les acusa de un complot para obstaculizar a un oficial federal.</p><p>Abughazaleh busca la nominación demócrata para el escaño del Distrito 9 de Illinois en las primarias del martes.</p><p>La noticia llega después de que los fiscales dijeran que planeaban reducir y revisar el caso de conspiración contra el grupo. Esto significa que Sharp y Walsh <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >se unirán a una lista cada vez mayor</a> de personas que han sido exoneradas después de ser acusadas de delitos no relacionados con la inmigración vinculados al Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>También es un golpe significativo para una acusación de alto perfil relacionada con la campaña de cumplimiento de inmigración.</p><p>Los fiscales federales en Chicago acusaron a 32 personas de delitos no relacionados con la inmigración conectados al Operativo Midway Blitz. Una vez que Perry conceda la nueva moción relacionada con Sharp y Walsh, 19 de los 32 habrán sido exonerados. Hasta ahora, ninguno de los 32 ha sido condenado.</p><p>Los acusados exonerados incluyen a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatoshttps://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >Marimar Martínez</a>, la mujer a la que un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza le disparó cinco veces en octubre pasado en el lado suroeste. Los fiscales abandonaron el caso en su contra en noviembre.</p><p>Un jurado también exoneró a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/23/jurado-rechaza-complot-de-asesinato-de-greg-bovino-y-absuelve-a-hombre-de-chicago" target="_blank" >Juan Espinoza Martinez</a> en enero después de haber sido acusado de ofrecer $10,000 por el asesinato del comandante de la Patrulla Fronteriza, Gregory Bovino. Las autoridades de inmigración detuvieron a Espinoza Martinez después de su absolución y lo han mantenido en custodia desde entonces.</p><p>Sin embargo, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/02/los-manifestantes-anti-ice-golpean-a-trump-en-la-corte-de-chicago-pero-todo-sigue-teniendo-un-costo" target="_blank" >el enjuiciamiento federal aún tiene un costo</a>, incluso cuando los cargos son finalmente abandonados. En el caso de Sharp, esto incluye la suspensión de su campaña para la Junta del Condado de Cook para que pudiera concentrarse en su defensa legal.</p><p>Los acusados de conspiración restantes de Broadview están programados para juicio el 26 de mayo. Los cargos giran en torno a eventos de la mañana del 26 de septiembre frente a la instalación de ICE en Broadview.</p><p>Una acusación alega que, mientras un agente conducía un vehículo hacia la instalación, los acusados y otros lo rodearon. Se alega que los miembros de la multitud golpearon el vehículo, lo empujaron, lo rayaron e incluso grabaron la palabra “PIG” (“puerco”) en él.</p><p>La multitud supuestamente rompió un espejo lateral y un limpiaparabrisas trasero y obligó al agente “a conducir a una velocidad extremadamente lenta”. Sin embargo, los fiscales acordaron recientemente eliminar cierto lenguaje de la acusación. También reconocieron durante una audiencia reciente que el período de la supuesta conspiración se limita al 26 de septiembre, que fue “espontánea” sin evidencia de un acuerdo explícito, y que todos los posibles co-conspiradores estaban presentes en Broadview en ese momento.</p><p>Los abogados defensores han señalado que un tema clave en el juicio podría ser la “decisión y motivación del agente para conducir su vehículo hacia una multitud de más de 50 individuos que protestaban pacíficamente por las atrocidades documentadas que ocurrían dentro de la instalación de Broadview”.</p><p>Han afirmado que sus clientes y otros estaban participando en una “Caminata de Jericó” supervisada por la policía local, que ayudó a que el tráfico pasara de manera segura a través de la manifestación.</p><p>Finalmente, un vehículo Ford Expedition conducido por un agente de ICE se volvió “directamente” hacia la multitud, según los documentos de defensa. No hay evidencia de que el agente de ICE esperara asistencia policial, sino que optó por “girar directamente hacia donde estaba reunida la multitud”, dicen los abogados defensores.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Gov. Pritzker wants funds flowing for Great Lakes carp plan after Trump heaps praise on project he paused
entries.summary: <p>Gov. JB Pritzker and Sen. Dick Durbin want President Donald Trump to put money where his mouth is on a massive project to help stop invasive carp from entering the Great Lakes — a project the Trump administration paused in December.</p><p>The president this week said “only Trump” can save the Great Lakes from invasive Asian carp and urged governors to “join in on the fight.” His post on Truth Social, which called it a “worthy cause,” came after a meeting with Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer. Trump referenced the governors of Illinois, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Pennsylvania, Ohio and New York in his post.</p><p>That social media commitment was surprising to Pritzker's office because the Trump administration in December issued an administrative review and paused the Brandon Road Interbasin Project, with no federal funds being delivered. It prompted Pritzker on Friday to once again demand the Trump administration release funds on the project.</p><p>“The Great Lakes are a treasured international resource and the largest fresh water source in the world, and if the Trump Administration does not deliver needed federal funds, then they are betraying every American that relies on this water,” Pritzker said in a statement. “It is imperative that President Trump uphold his stated commitments to stop the invasive species threatening our Great Lakes and release the funds needed to resume construction on the Brandon Road Interbasin Project. Illinois has always done our part, and it is past time President Trump do his.”</p><p>Only Illinois and Michigan have directed resources to the project, but Pritzker's office on Friday said it "would welcome additional commitments from the full force of all eight Great Lake states."</p><p>The $1.15 billion project in the Des Plaines River near Joliet is seen as a vital way to stop invasive Asian carp from entering the Great Lakes. Without a barrier, the carp could decimate the $20 billion fishing and boating industries and upend the freshwater ecosystem. The species has already made it into the Illinois River from the Mississippi River. Fishermen contracted by the state of Illinois have removed nearly 78 million pounds of invasive carp from waterways, according to Pritzker's office. The Great Lakes are also a fresh drinking water source for more than 40 million Americans. </p><p>In addition to the Brandon Road project funding pause, three major federal grants to Illinois totaling $13.6 million for invasive carp removal are paused pending review by the U.S. Department of the Interior.</p><p>Durbin on the Senate floor Wednesday called the carp issue “a serious threat to the future of the Great Lakes,” and said Trump needs to get on board with the project as soon as possible.</p><p>“We have a new means, an effective means, to stop this fish from invading the Great Lakes. We need the approval of President Trump to make that happen," Durbin said. "Yesterday, he promised that he would be part of the solution. Today, I’m asking him to really step forward. Make a call today — not tomorrow — make a call today to the Army Corps of Engineers to tell them you’re lifting this Trump Administration pause on the development of Brandon Road."</p><p>Trump in May 2025 also issued a memorandum to protect the Great Lakes from invasive carp species. The commitment came after Illinois demanded a pledge that the government would provide $225 million that was already appropriated by Congress. Illinois never received that money, despite his commitment.</p><p>In February 2025, the governor's office postponed a “property rights closing” on the project, “based on the anticipated lack of federal funding for the project.” The governor’s office said the state would only move forward with funding once it received assurance from the federal government that federal funds are secure — and that the Trump administration wasn't going to try to tinker with their agreement. At that time, the Trump administration had already withheld $117 million in federal grants to the Illinois Department of Natural Resources, which has forced the state to pause contracts and put nearly 70 infrastructure projects on hold.</p><p>The Asian carp dilemma made its way to "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert" on Thursday night, with the show opening to a satire declaring, "Trump is waging war on a violent and destructive specifies of fish in the Great Lakes known as the Asian carp." </p><p>"Donald Trump has found the next threat to America, the Asian carp, which can only mean one thing: Operation Epic Fishy," a narrator said. </p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: CPS Board names 3 finalists to lead district: Interim CEO King, former NYC, East Baton Rouge schools chiefs
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education announced three finalists to run the school district Friday.</p><p>One surprising name on the list: Macquline King, who has been serving as interim leader of Chicago Public Schools since June, after originally being told she was not in the running.</p><p>She joins Meisha Porter, the first Black woman to lead New York City’s school system, and Sito Narcisse, a former superintendent of Louisiana’s East Baton Rouge Parish schools and chief of secondary schools in the District of Columbia. Narcisse is the son of Haitian immigrants.</p><p>Next week, the finalists will meet with Mayor Brandon Johnson and will be interviewed by a panel of 15 that includes students, parents, teachers and community members.</p><p>The announcement comes after a months-long search process that encountered several hiccups.</p><p>Originally, school board members did not plan to make the names of the finalists public because the search firm they hired said that would discourage candidates from applying. But in November, Porter and another candidate's name became public, and that candidate dropped out of contention.</p><p>At that point, the search was re-opened and board members conducted a new round of interviews.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c80000" name="image-c80000"></a> class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brittainy Newman/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The fact that King was not a finalist in November became a point of contention. The West Side NAACP and some school board members suggested that she be kept on until at least January 2027 — when the board will go from being partially appointed by the mayor to being fully elected by voters.</p><p>King, a former CPS principal who previously served in the mayor’s office, had irked some board members by not going along with the budget plan put forth by the mayor, which included the possibility of borrowing.</p><p>But this fall she seemed to gain some respect among board members for getting the district organized to respond to federal immigration enforcement.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://alumni.mercy.edu/meisha-ross-porter" target="_blank" ><u>Porter was born and raised</u></a> in New York City and attended public schools there. She graduated from Queens Vocational and Technical High School and went on to receive a bachelor of arts degree in English concentrating in cross cultural literature and Black and Puerto Rican studies at Hunter College.</p><p>Porter was the first Black woman to lead New York City’s school system, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.nyssba.org/about-nyssba/meisha-ross-porter-area-13-director" target="_blank" ><u>her biography</u></a> with the New York State School Boards Association.</p><p>Narcisse served as the superintendent of East Baton Rouge Parish schools in Louisiana from 2021 through 2024, according to his LinkedIn profile.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3155374/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1162x652+0+271/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F92%2F38af7509442684a2988b1ebca101%2Fsito-narcisse.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sito Narcisse previously served as a top schools official in East Baton Rouge Parish, Louisiana, Washington, D.C., and Nashville, Tennessee.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of SJ Apple Group</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>He currently works with SJ Apple Group, a company he founded which works with K-12 school districts to develop “data-driven solutions that improve student outcomes and address the unique needs of today’s learners,” <a class="Link" href="https://www.sjapple.com/about" target="_blank" ><u>according to its website</u></a>.</p><p>Narcisse grew up speaking Creole and French in New York. He went through the public school system there while learning English. He eventually went on to graduate from Kennesaw State University in Georgia with a degree in French and then went on to earn a master’s of education from Vanderbilt University and a doctorate in education administration from the University of Pittsburgh.</p><p>In an interview with an <a class="Link" href="https://dmj.dmgroupk12.com/articles/taking-on-the-challenges-of-urban-education" target="_blank" ><u>education management group</u></a>, he said public education has “always been my passion” and added that for him “it’s always been about impact, specifically on Black and Brown kids, because I’m a product of that growing up in New York.”</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Chicago murals: Friends paint McKinley Park viaduct to prevent future graffiti
entries.summary: <p>Ashley Freda had one overarching goal when she reached out to her friend and muralist who goes by the artist name Ocean: Break the cycle of tagging and painting over and tagging again on the viaduct near her home in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/mckinley-park" >McKinley Park</a>.</p><p>“This vicious viaduct was getting tagged and buffed within 24 hours. It would happen over and over again,” Freda says. “Sometimes the best way to stop a wall from being erased and tagged over and over again is to make something people want to protect.”</p><p>So Freda, whose artist name is Agua Girl, reached out to Ocean, with whom she had collaborated on a mural in West Town. She asked if he’d be interested in doing another. Ocean, who grew up in McKinley Park and adjacent Brighton Park, readily agreed.</p><p>“We’re both very much of and from the neighborhood,” he says.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-810000" name="image-810000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1281005/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5495x3084+0+291/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F30%2F906dfe16409bb8cb05f8d452b3b7%2Fmurals-brighton-260315-002.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source
