data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/decde61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5495x3084+0+291/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F30%2F906dfe16409bb8cb05f8d452b3b7%2Fmurals-brighton-260315-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ashley Freda, who goes by the artist name "Agua Girl," and an artist who goes by "Ocean" stand in front of their McKinley Park viaduct mural." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/decde61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5495x3084+0+291/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F30%2F906dfe16409bb8cb05f8d452b3b7%2Fmurals-brighton-260315-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c678f78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5495x3084+0+291/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F30%2F906dfe16409bb8cb05f8d452b3b7%2Fmurals-brighton-260315-002.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/decde61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5495x3084+0+291/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F30%2F906dfe16409bb8cb05f8d452b3b7%2Fmurals-brighton-260315-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ashley Freda, who goes by the artist name “Agua Girl,” and an artist who goes by “Ocean” finished a two-panel spread on a McKinley Park viaduct in 2024 and hope to keep expanding the mural.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The duo spent two weeks brainstorming, sketching, creating concepts and collecting inspiration. The resulting wall is a two-panel spread under the viaduct at West 35th and South Leavitt streets in McKinley Park. They finished it in 2024 and hope to keep expanding the mural.</p><p>“Our neighborhood, they have a real appetite for art, engagement and connection. At times we lack so much here that people don’t even understand how they can participate in street art,” Ocean says.</p><p>Added Freda, “being able to activate walls on the South Side is something Ocean and I are constantly trying to participate in.”</p><p>The walls are a complete collaboration between the two artists, with flowers native to McKinley Park spread throughout. Ocean painted the fish and the frogs, while Freda added the female faces.</p><p>The first panel features an angel fish staring at a feminine face: “It’s very isolated. Who are you and who am I? There’s heaps of complexity,” Ocean says.</p><p>But in the second panel, “suddenly you’re in the thick of it, and there’s light everywhere you look and color and saturation.”</p><p>“The broad story we’re aiming to tell is the journey of discovery and what can be seen if you stop and take a moment,” he says.</p><p>Like, for example, their art.</p><p>Freda and Ocean used spray paint to create for the entire mural, they say. While Freda brought her perspective from living nearby, Ocean says painting the mural brought up his memories as a kid in the neighborhood. It’s received a warm welcome from neighbors, they say.</p><p>The mural isn’t alone in the viaduct. Other murals honoring the Chicago Park District and Chicago firefighters are on the facing wall across the street, with portions tagged and buffed there, too. A Samurai mural by Chicago artist James Sturnfield is farther down the wall and remains largely intact.</p><p>Freda says she plans to continue painting murals around McKinley Park and Bridgeport, South Side neighborhoods she says desperately need them.</p><p>The two artists are planning more collaboration murals together. “At the end of the day, it’s like tangled headphones in your pocket,” Ocean says, of their teamwork.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-fullBleed> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6f0000" entries.title: Rule changes for SNAP squeeze older Chicagoans
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p> 🔎 <b>Below</b>: New work requirements for federal food assistance benefits put pressure on Chicago-area elders.</p><p> 🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: The rich history of Chicago's oldest Black church, why hailstones that fell Tuesday were so big and more news you need to know.</p><p><b>📝 Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-j/" target="_blank" >Northwestern falls to Purdue</a>, 81-68; <a class="Link" Kimbark Avenue in Woodlawn, Feb. 25, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-h/" target="_blank" >Older Chicagoans are at high risk of losing SNAP as new work rules kick in</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-k/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>Bootstrapping</b>: Older adults are at risk of losing SNAP food benefits after work requirements were expanded to include 55- to 64-year-olds, part of President Donald Trump’s sweeping tax overhaul. In Chicago, only 35% of households that included adults in this age group were working at least 20 hours per week, according to a Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ analysis of data from the U.S. Census 2024 American Community Survey.</p><p><b>The stakes</b>: The new requirements call for people to work or volunteer 80 hours a month, which translates to an average four hours per weekday. The rules also expanded to include veterans and parents whose youngest child is 14 years or older. If SNAP recipients don’t meet the rules or get an exemption by May, they will begin losing benefits.</p><p><b>Uphill battles</b>: Older people who have been out of the workforce might find it harder to compete for jobs, especially because of continuing ageism, advocates say. This group also often spends time looking after relatives, making it harder to meet the 80 hours each month. In response, community groups across Chicago are expanding volunteer opportunities.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/13/older-chicagoans-high-risk-of-losing-snap-food-assistance-new-work-rules-kick-in-55-64-may" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>EDUCATION 📚</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0e0000" name="image-0e0000"></a> <picture href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-b/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Hands-on learning</b>: The Chicago Teachers Union intends to launch what amounts to a one-day strike May 1, and is asking the mayor and school board for support so students can miss class to participate. CTU wants members and students to take part in "age-appropriate" civic education, labor history programs, voter registration, know-your-rights training, rallies and marches.</p><p><b>Main messages</b>: Union leaders' reasons to participate include to demand the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency get "out of our cities" and to "tax the rich to support our schools and vital services." They also say public education is facing attacks from "MAGA politicians" who support policies of both President Trump and "corporate interests."</p><p><b>A precedent</b>: In 2016, teachers staged a one-day strike in support of higher wages and more state funding. That action led CPS to cancel school for the day and to set up contingency sites where parents who needed to work could drop off their children. CPS filed a charge with the Illinois Educational Labor Relations Board alleging the strike was illegal.</p><p><a class="Link" target="_blank" >How Quinn Chapel AME put its faith in action</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-q/" target="_blank" >Nicole Jeanine Johnson</a></h5><p><b>Community heart</b>: By the 1840s, free Blacks flocked to Chicago to steer clear of slavery. Some of them started Quinn Chapel AME, the city’s first Black church, and its physical building served as a beacon for freedom seekers. As the law closed free Blacks off from public society, the church became the center of the community. It was where people learned to read, engaged in debate club, and had women’s Bible study and singing classes.</p><p><b>Underground Railroad station</b>: Those who led Quinn Chapel’s emancipation work were among the city’s Black elite — leaders in the business community and in politics. But the veil of class, wealth and mixed ancestry didn't fool church members into thinking they couldn’t be returned to slavery or taken into bondage for the first time. The chapel's location along the Chicago River, with close proximity to Lake Michigan, made it a strategic station on the Underground Railroad.</p><p><b>Living history</b>: Inside the church is a massive organ purchased from the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition German pavilion. Members at the time held the first ever June Rose benefit concert to pay for the instrument. Since then, Quinn Chapel has hosted the concerts for more than 130 years. "It’s kind of a homecoming for everybody," Senior Pastor Troy Venning said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-jr/" target="_blank" >Sixth grader wins CPS spelling bee</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtriuhd-tlkjhhnv-jy/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a></h5><p>Less studying, better results: That was Seraj Zerhouni’s strategy at the Chicago Public Schools Spelling Bee Championship on Thursday.</p><p>Despite months of studying difficult words, Seraj lost last year’s citywide spelling bee by misspelling "Tiffany." The LaSalle II Magnet School sixth grader returned this year with a simpler approach — prepare a little less and study easier words.</p><p>It worked. Seraj advanced steadily through the early rounds, then won the championship in Round 18, outlasting 48 other CPS students.</p><p>In May, he will compete at the Scripps National Spelling Bee in Washington.</p><p>"It feels like redemption," Seraj said. "I was pretty disappointed last year that I didn’t get so far, and now I’m, like, determined to get far, and then I got far."</p><p>Seraj took the title after correctly spelling "logothete," which means an administrator in the Byzantine Empire.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/12/cps-spelling-bee-winner-seraj-zerhouni-winner-sixth-grader-lasalle-ii-magnet" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h3><p><b>Do you have a favorite local craft beer? Tell us about it.</b></p><p>Reply to this email (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <u>What kind of museum is missing from Chicago? What should it cover?</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“A ‘Lucas Museum of Narrative Art’ … oh wait.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Robert Juergens</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“A Chicago Blues Museum, preferably at the former Chess Records location on South Michigan Avenue.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Richard Bartecki</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“It needs the DePaul Art Museum, which [DePaul University] is closing.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Amy M. Tyson</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“An offshoot or partner of the National Comedy Center ... specifically for Chicago comedy.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Scotty Jay</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“Museum of Unknowns, highlighting nonfamous, everyday people doing big or important things in the city. Rotate selected individuals monthly!”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Kimberly Conner</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.author: Esther Bergdahl | WBEZ

entries.title: Man fatally shot in Garfield Park neighborhood
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police are investigating a Garfield Park neighborhood shooting that left a 23-year-old man dead earlier this week. </p><p>Taeshaun Conic, 23, was gunned down while walking about 11:40 a.m. Wednesday in the 4100 block of West Adams Street, police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>A Thursday autopsy determined Conic, who was pronounced dead at Mount Sinai Hospital, died of multiple gunshot wounds and his death was ruled a homicide, the medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Nobody else was hurt and the shooter fled west and was not in custody. </p><p>Area 4 detectives are investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: O'Hare reports long delays due to wind, radar issue
entries.summary: <p>Planes arriving at O'Hare International Airport were experiencing long delays Friday morning due to heavy wind that disrupted the airport's radar.</p><p>Gusts over 60 mph were pummeling the Chicago area, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/13/winds-nearing-60-mph-may-knock-down-trees-power-lines-and-make-driving-very-difficult-in-chicago-area" target="_blank" >disrupting transportation across the region</a>.</p><p>The delays at O'Hare began around 5 a.m. due to high wind, according to a Federal Aviation Administration alert. A ground stop was issued around 6 a.m., diverting planes from the airport for about an hour. </p><p>As of 10 a.m., the FAA reported arrival delays at O'Hare averaging 85 minutes due to an "equipment outage." </p><p>An FAA spokesperson said the agency was slowing departures at O'Hare "due to a ground radar issue caused by heavy winds."</p><p>"Technicians are working to address the problem," the spokesperson said in an email.</p><p>The Chicago Department of Aviation, which runs the city's airports, was reporting average arrival delays at O'Hare at 58 minutes. Departures at the airport were averaging 27 minutes.</p><p>Midway International Airport, about 20 miles south of O'Hare, was doing better. The airport was reporting delays under 15 minutes for arrivals and delays, according to the city.</p><p>The Chicago area is under a high wind warning until 4 p.m. Friday, according to the National Weather Service. A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at O'Hare earlier in the morning, according to the agency.</p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Bears lose safety Jonathan Owens to Colts
entries.summary: <p>Jonathan Owens, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> safety best-known for his marriage to superstar gymnast Simone Biles, is leaving Chicago. Owens agreed to sign a one-year deal with the Colts, a source confirmed Friday.</p><p>Owens spent two years with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >Bears</a>, starting five games in 2024. He was a special teams contributor, playing 55% of the team’s kicking downs in 2024 and 58% in 2025. The Bears’ safeties room will look very different next year after starters Kevin Byard and Jaquan Brisker agreed to sign one-year deals with the Patriots and Steelers, respectively.</p><p>Byard has been his wife’s biggest fan since their 2023 marriage. In the summer of 2024, the Bears let Owens briefly leave training camp to travel to Paris to watch his wife in the Summer Olympics. The two went to the Winter Olympics as fans earlier this year.</p><p>“Overjoyed for her,” he said upon his return. “All of her accomplishments that she's made. Just being a face for gymnastics, man. She became the most decorated American gymnast ever. So it was amazing that I was able to be there and witness that for her.”<br>Biles has 11 Olympic medals and is considered the greatest female gymnast of all time.<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Leo senior Michael Lewis' fantastic year: 'America's Got Talent' and the IHSA basketball state finals
entries.summary: <p>CHAMPAIGN—Leo’s young basketball team held its own with heavily-favored East St. Louis for three quarters on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-boys-basketball-state-finals-scores-illinois" >Friday</a> at State Farm Center, but the Flyers eventually pulled away for a 59-40 win in the Class 3A semifinals.</p><p>The Champaign experienced topped off an incredible senior year for Leo’s Michael Lewis, who is a member of the all-boys school’s famous choir.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2025/09/24/chicago-leo-catholic-high-school-choir-americas-got-talent" >Lewis traveled to California multiple times to perform on “America’s Got Talent.”</a> The Leo choir finished fourth in the country in the televised competition. Then in January Lewis <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/2026/01/10/leo-basketball-chicago-yorkville-christian" >drained a 40-foot buzzer-beater game-winner</a> to beat Yorkville Christian.</p><p>But Lewis said the trip to Champaign and the experience playing at State Farm Center beats it all.</p><p>“AGT was amazing but in all my four years of high school [basketball] I had never been anywhere past the regional finals, so this is an amazing experience for me,” Lewis said. “I will cherish this more than America’s Got Talent.”</p><p>Lewis played three minutes off the bench for the Lions (27-7). Junior Brian Kizer led Leo with 11 points. His feelings about the day are similar to Lewis’s.</p><p>“It is an absolutely wonderful feeling,” Kizer said. “I am very grateful for this moment. It didn’t turn out how we wanted to but I’m grateful.”</p><p>East St. Louis (32-4), led by 6-8 Penn State recruit Jamison White, has been the best team in Class 3A all season. White finished with 12 points and 15 rebounds. Alex Johnson added 19 points and 10 rebounds for the Flyers.</p><p>A three-pointer by 6-8 freshman Elon Henderson pulled Leo within 37-31 at the end of the third quarter. East St. Louis ripped off a 14-2 run to open the fourth quarter and put the game away.</p><p>“Obviously it wasn’t the outcome we planned for,” Lions coach Jimalle Ridley said. “We really had our sights set on leaving here with the first-place trophy. The energy and effort they put into this is unbelievable. The improvements we’ve made just shows the character of these guys.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a He was 2-for-4 from three-point range.</p><p>“I trust everyone on the team to look for me and when they find me, I have to make my moment,” Henderson said.</p><p>“[Henderson’s] grade level is freshman but mentally he’s older,” Ridley said “He’s going to be one of the top players in the country one day. He’s about to have a heck of a summer. He’s playing for Bradley Beal Elite.”</p><p>Leo starts five juniors and has two freshmen and a junior in its rotation.</p><p>“We got fatigued,” Ridley said. We got tired with that big floor and battling with a guy like [White]. And our depth is not our greatest asset. But we are a very young team. I know we are going to be a pretty tough team to beat in the future.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-960000" name="html-embed-module-960000"></a><blockquote class="tiktok-embed" cite="https://www.tiktok.com/@michaelsobriensports/video/7616844749013585182" data-video-id="7616844749013585182" style="max-width: 605px;min-width: 325px;" > <section> <a target="_blank" title="@michaelsobriensports" href="https://www.tiktok.com/@michaelsobriensports?refer=embed">@michaelsobriensports</a> Leo senior Michael Lewis performed on America’s Got Talent and played in the IHSA basketball state finals this year. <a title="basketball" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/basketball?refer=embed">#basketball</a> <a title="ihsa" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/ihsa?refer=embed">#ihsa</a> <a title="chicago" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/chicago?refer=embed">#chicago</a> <a title="leo" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/leo?refer=embed">#leo</a> <a target="_blank" title="♬ original sound - michaelsobriensports" href="https://www.tiktok.com/music/original-sound-7616844749190335262?refer=embed">♬ original sound - michaelsobriensports</a> </section> </blockquote> <script async src="https://www.tiktok.com/embed.js"></script> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: Threat deemed 'non credible' at Lane Tech, school says
entries.summary: <p>A potential threat called in to Lane Tech College Prep that prompted visits from the police and fire departments has been deemed 'non-credible' and no students or staff are in any danger, the school said. </p><p>At 1:25 p.m. firefighters were called to the school, 2501 W. Addison St., for a call about an 'unknown object,' said Chicago Fire Department spokesperson Larry Merritt. A Level 1 hazardous materials call for the object was "secured" at 1:44 p.m., he added.</p><p>Merritt said he didn't know why the object was suspicious.</p><p>The school was placed on soft lockdown after receiving a phone call from an unknown source that included a potential threat to our school, Lane Tech principal Edwina Thompson said in a letter sent to parents and guardians. </p><p>Police later determined the potential threat was "non-credible, Thompson said in the statement. </p><p>"There is no safety threat to our school, and everyone is safe. All after-school activities are expected to proceed as planned."</p><p>Chicago police had no further details immediately. </p><p>According to police radio transmissions, the call happened during sixth period, a "soft lockdown" was put in place, and some of the students were moved to another building.</p><p>A Chicago police officer on the scene reported to an OEMC dispatcher they were detaining someone after a backpack was found in the men's washroom on the second floor of the school. </p><p>“I have him in my custody,’’ the officer told the dispatcher. “There’s a backpack…I’ve not heard any noises or sounds from the backpack.”</p><p>A Chicago Public Schools representative did not immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: White Sox to be 'mindful of the long term' with Kyle Teel's recovery from right hamstring strain
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Not even a Grade 2 hamstring strain could put <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> catcher Kyle Teel — a notoriously upbeat player — in a dour mood.</p><p>Teel returned to Arizona on Thursday and spoke to the media Friday after injuring his hamstring running the bases on a sixth-inning double Tuesday night in Team Italy’s 8-6 World Baseball Classic win over Team USA. He’s already begun treatment for the injury, which general manager Chris Getz estimated to be between four to six weeks.</p><p>"Those are just real estimates," manager Will Venable said. "Hopefully it’s quicker. It could be longer. I expressed to Kyle: [A] hamstring [injury], you want to put those in the rearview mirror and make sure as you progress you are being really mindful of the long term here. "</p><p>Teel said as he rounded first, he looked to see where the ball was, and he “kicked it in another gear” once he saw how close the outfielder was to the ball.</p><p>“I just felt it tightened up right away,” Teel said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">White Sox and Team Italy catcher Kyle Teel is leaving with an injury. You could see him in pain just after he rounded first base <a href="https://t.co/wOXUqINGgA">pic.twitter.com/wOXUqINGgA</a></p>— Talkin' Baseball (@TalkinBaseball_) <a href="https://twitter.com/TalkinBaseball_/status/2031562728024752199?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 11, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>After a slow spring training — Teel batted .071 with a .378 OPS over five games — he turned it on at the WBC with four hits over two games. Teel was impressed with teammate Sam Antonacci, who’s having a breakout performance for Italy.</p><p>“That team [was] so much fun to be a part of,” Teel said. “[Sox prospect Sam Antonacci] is a dog. He just surprised me so much. He’s just such a great player, and for him to never play in the big leagues and go out on that stage and leave it out there like he did and how he’s going to on Sunday, it's unbelievable."</p><p>Royals first baseman Vinnie Pasquantino naturally assumed a leadership role as one of the most accomplished players on the team, and his leadership style made an impression on Teel.</p><p>With the clubhouse skewing younger, Teel is positioned as one of the core players going forward — he can have a more vocal leadership role once he returns from his injury.</p><p>“He has a way of making people feel included but also making sure people get their work done,” Teel said. “He can be light but also be serious. Being with that group, I learned a lot. “</p><p>Teel has already put the injury behind him. He’s focused on attacking his rehab to return when he’s ready. The Sox have the luxury of being able to slow his return because they have two capable catchers in Korey Lee and Edgar Quero.</p><p>“We’ll be mindful [of] his rehab, but that he’ll be back in no time,” Venable said.</p><p>But despite the injury setback, Teel’s time with Italy was fruitful for his development as a hitter and leader. He said that he felt he was hitting balls hard in Arizona but wasn’t having success. But in his two games with Italy, Teel said his approach came together and that he enjoyed seeing his success pay off on a bigger stage. That stretch with Italy had a profound effect on Teel and will be helpful even as he goes through rehab.</p><p>Being around a former major-league catcher like Francisco Cervelli — Italy's manager — was helpful in Teel’s work behind the plate. While injured, Venable said Teel can still dive into opposing hitters' reports and continue building relationships with pitchers.</p><p>“The way I look at it is, ‘How can I go through this and come back better than I was before?'" Teel said. "That’s my number one goal. I’m feeling better, but I want to come out of this stronger than before.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Remaining Broadview 6 defendants want records showing Trump administration's influence on conspiracy case
entries.summary: <p>Four demonstrators who say they’ve been targeted for conspiracy charges because they spoke out against the Trump administration want a judge<b> </b>to force prosecutors<b> </b>to hand over records that could show the White House<b> </b>improperly influenced their<b> </b>indictment.</p><p>A new 26-page motion<b> </b>lays out a case<b> </b>that the charges leveled against the group, including congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh, is another example of President Donald Trump's administration using the Justice Department to target his “perceived political enemies.”</p><p>“The record readily shows how the Trump administration and its allies have expressly targeted Ms. Abughazaleh on numerous occasions in response to her public criticisms of it and its aggressive immigration enforcement tactics,” the defense attorneys wrote.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" >Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Abughazaleh is running in Tuesday’s primary for the Democratic nomination<b> </b>for Illinois’ 9th Congressional District.</p><p>But the new motion also points to the activism of Abughazaleh’s co-defendants. They include Oak Park village trustee Brian Straw, 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael Rabbitt and Andre Martin, a senior member of Abughazaleh’s campaign.</p><p>The motion comes one day after prosecutors <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >dropped<b> </b>charges against two</a> of the original six defendants in the case: Joselyn<b> </b>Walsh, a musician, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/06/cook-county-board-candidate-suspends-campaign-to-fight-indictment" target="_blank" >Catherine “Cat” Sharp</a>, a Democrat who dropped her bid for Cook County Board<b> </b>in Tuesday’s primary<b> </b>to fight the charges.</p><p>The conspiracy case is set for trial May 26. The charges revolve around events on the morning of Sept. 26<b> </b>outside the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement<b> </b>facility<b> </b>in Broadview. Prosecutors on Friday said they intend to narrow the case to claims that the group conspired to prevent, "by force and intimidation," a federal agent from discharging his duties.</p><div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Congressional candidate Kat Abughazaleh, facing charges of conspiracy for protest actions at the Broadview Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility, speaks at a press conference at Federal Plaza, Wednesday on Nov. 12, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Tim</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Defense attorneys asked U.S. District Judge<b> </b>April Perry<b> </b>on Friday<b> </b>to force prosecutors<b> </b>to turn over records relating to “any improper influence exerted upon the U.S. Attorney’s office” that was tied to the targeting of the four defendants.</p><p>They also sought a federal agent’s<b> </b>training records<b> </b>and internal communications<b> </b>between the feds related to crowd management<b> </b>outside the Broadview facility.</p><p>The defense attorneys included a photo of Trump’s image<b> </b>at the front entryway to the Justice Department’s<b> </b>headquarters<b> </b>in Washington, D.C.,<b> </b>alleging the department is being used to retaliate against Trump's enemies.</p><p>The attorneys noted that far-right<b> </b>influencer<b> </b>Laura Loomer, who reportedly has the president’s ear, “praised”<b> </b>an agent<b> </b>who body-slammed<b> </b>Abughazaleh<b> </b>during a Sept. 19<b> </b>protest<b> </b>outside the ICE facility.</p><p>They also pointed to media reports placing Abughazaleh on a so-called “Enemies List.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2d0000" name="image-2d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An image from an MS NOW report that depicts congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh on a so-called Justice Department “Enemies List.” It was included in a motion filed Friday by lawyers for Abughazaleh and three others charged in a conspiracy tied to a protest in suburban Broadview.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court records</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Straw<b> </b>has spoken out against ICE as well,<b> </b>including by seeking termination<b> </b>of Oak Park’s contract<b> </b>for Flock's automated license plate readers, according to the motion.</p><p>He did so while noting<b> </b>that the data they collect<b> </b>has been used by ICE<b> </b>for immigration enforcement, the attorneys wrote.</p><p>Rabbitt was also among 47 congressional, state and local elected officials who signed a letter<b> </b>denouncing violence<b> </b>by federal agents, they noted.</p><p>The indictment in the case<b> </b>alleges that, while a federal agent<b> </b>drove a vehicle<b> </b>toward the Broadview facility, the defendants and others<b> </b>surrounded it. Members of the crowd<b> </b>allegedly<b> </b>banged on the vehicle, pushed against it, scratched it and even etched<b> </b>the word<b> </b>“PIG”<b> </b>into it.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/12/kat-abughazaleh-operation-midway-blitz-donald-trump-jb-pritzker-brandon-johnson-other-candidates-face-arraignment-in-broadview-conspiracy-case" >‘We will win': Six Broadview protesters plead not guilty to conspiracy, decry attack on First Amendment</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The crowd allegedly broke a side mirror and a rear windshield wiper<b> </b>and forced<b> </b>the agent “to drive at an extremely slow rate of speed.”</p><p>The defense attorneys<b> </b>insisted Friday<b> </b>that none of their clients “utilized or exerted any force against the agent. No defendant threatened the agent or made any threat whatsoever. No defendant did anything to intimidate the agent.</p><p>“No defendant scratched the vehicle,”<b> </b>they wrote. “No defendant etched the word ‘Pig’ onto the vehicle. No defendant pulled the wiper blade off the vehicle.”</p><p>“The one commonality among these four defendants,” they insisted, “is that via social media platforms and public statements they were all outspoken critics of the Trump administration.”</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Trinity dancers delight as St. Patrick's Day nears
entries.summary: <p>Dancers from the Trinity Irish Dancing Academy were busy entertaining students and staffers at elementary schools in the East Village and Portage Park neighborhoods Friday morning to celebrate the upcoming St. Patrick's Day holiday. </p><p>The troupe danced away at LaSalle II Magnet School, 1148 N. Honore St. in the East Village neighborhood, and Prussing Elementary School, 4650 N. Menard Ave. in Portage Park. </p><p>For over 40 years, the Chicago, Milwaukee, and Madison institution has become the most widely recognized Irish dance program in the world, according to <a class="Link" href="https://trinityirishdance.com/" target="_blank" >their website. </a>Their mission, according to the site, is elevating children, the community, and the world through the power and grace of Irish dance.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-830000" name="image-830000"></a> entries.title: Grupos comunitarios quieren que CPS trabaje para prevenir bajas en asistencia por actividad federal de inmigración
entries.summary: <p>Una coalición de grupos comunitarios y una organización de defensa de padres están instando a las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) a tomar medidas ahora para prevenir descensos en la asistencia en caso de que haya un nuevo aumento en la aplicación de la ley de inmigración federal.</p><p>Entre las cosas que están pidiendo: actualizaciones más frecuentes sobre las tendencias de asistencia y espacios seguros designados donde los estudiantes y padres puedan refugiarse si se encuentran con una amenaza al ir o volver de la escuela.</p><p>“Se necesitaba mucho para nuestras familias para lo que no estábamos preparados”, dijo Ana Lorenzana del Northwest Center en Belmont Cragin, quien habló el miércoles durante una sesión informativa virtual sobre <a class="Link" href="https://kidsfirstchicago.org/assets/miscellaneous/2026-K1C-Safe-Passage-report-v5.pdf" target="_blank" >un informe que su organización y varias otras trabajaron para realizarlo</a>.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-850000" name="module-850000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/11/a-pesar-de-las-advertencias-no-hay-senales-de-aumento-de-la-presencia-de-ice-esta-primavera" target="_blank" >No está claro si el presidente Donald Trump</a> aumentará el número de agentes federales en Chicago y si empleará el tipo de tácticas agresivas que devastaron a las comunidades inmigrantes con miedo el otoño pasado. Por ahora, no hay señales de un aumento en primavera, ya que estas tácticas están bajo escrutinio, y altos funcionarios como el comandante de la Patrulla Fronteriza, Gregory Bovino, ya no están en sus puestos.</p><p>Pero si sucede, el sistema escolar necesita estar preparado, a diferencia del otoño cuando la respuesta fue, según el informe, “desigual, frágil e insostenible”.</p><p>“La buena noticia es que tenemos tiempo para prepararnos”, dijo Daniel Anello, director ejecutivo de la organización de defensa de padres Kids First Chicago, que lideró el informe. “La mala noticia es que no tenemos demasiado tiempo para prepararnos, dada nuestra situación, y realmente necesitamos organizarnos”.</p><p>Kids First Chicago se asoció con la <a class="Link" href="https://authenticengagement.info/" target="_blank" >Coalition for Authentic Community Engagement</a>, un grupo que incluye a pastores de iglesias afroamericanas prominentes y organizaciones que sirven o representan a muchas de las comunidades inmigrantes de la ciudad, incluidos los estadounidenses de origen chino y los refugiados de Medio Oriente.</p><p>Los funcionarios de CPS dijeron que aprecian el enfoque del informe en la colaboración y evaluarán sus recomendaciones.</p><p>“Seguimos comprometidos a trabajar junto a todos los socios para fortalecer nuestras prácticas y asegurar que educadores y estudiantes puedan prosperar en un entorno seguro”, escribió un portavoz de CPS en un comunicado.</p><p>Si bien CPS ha declarado regularmente que la asistencia general no sufrió mucho este otoño, los autores del informe llegaron a una conclusión similar a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/13/como-fue-que-los-agentes-de-inmigracion-alteraron-las-escuelas-de-cps-este-otono" target="_blank" >un análisis de WBEZ y Sun-Times a principios de este año escolar</a>: Hubo picos en el ausentismo en días y semanas particulares.</p><p>El ausentismo más alto de lo habitual entre los estudiantes de inglés como segundo idioma fue un problema particular, encontró el grupo. En la séptima semana de clases, estimaron que 3,000 más estudiantes de inglés se quedaron en casa en comparación con años anteriores.</p><p>Marlon Henríquez, director de la Academia Scholastic Gunsaulus, una escuela magnet en el vecindario del lado suroeste, Brighton Park, dijo que una vez que la actividad de ejecución federal disminuyó, la asistencia se recuperó en la escuela primaria.</p><p>“Nuestras familias quieren venir a la escuela”, comentó. “Eso es muy claro”.</p><p>Pero la asistencia entre los estudiantes de secundaria que aprenden inglés no se ha recuperado completamente, dijo Anello. Dijo que “hay más trabajo por hacer” para volver a involucrar a esos estudiantes.</p><p>El informe presenta varias recomendaciones, incluyendo priorizar el apoyo a comunidades donde ha habido mucha actividad de agentes de inmigración y, por consiguiente, alto ausentismo.</p><p>En estas comunidades, especialmente, el informe solicita a CPS que publique informes semanales de asistencia para que los problemas se detecten más rápidamente.</p><p>Los autores del informe dicen que los directores y otro personal de alto nivel deberían recibir <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/05/voluntarios-patrullan-pilsen-buscando-alertar-sobre-presencia-de-agentes" target="_blank" >entrenamiento de respuesta rápida</a> y que las escuelas deberían tener un equipo de llegada y despedida con un líder designado que pueda asegurarse de que se siga un protocolo cuando haya amenazas de agentes federales cerca.</p><p>Kids First y los grupos comunitarios también quieren que CPS haga más para llevar a los estudiantes a la escuela. Señalan que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/13/como-fue-que-los-agentes-de-inmigracion-alteraron-las-escuelas-de-cps-este-otono" target="_blank" >las comunidades respondieron este otoño</a>, proveyendo autobuses escolares caminando, transporte compartido y vigilias vecinales. Pero dicen que el distrito necesita un enfoque más coordinado.</p><p>Henríquez dijo que donde vive en el lado norte, muchos residentes salieron a vigilar a los niños mientras iban y venían de la escuela. Pero Gunsaulus está localizada en una área llena de inmigrantes y residentes que estaban demasiado asustados para estar afuera y ser esos ojos y oídos.</p><p>“Llegar a la escuela y salir de la escuela, eso es algo que está fuera de nuestro control”, dijo. “Tener individuos presentes, abriendo puertas, diciendo buenos días, despidiéndolos, siendo una cara conocida y feliz, tendría un gran impacto en la comunidad de Gunsaulus”.</p><p>Los grupos dicen que CPS debería revertir <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/08/13/cps-safe-passage" target="_blank" >recortes recientes al programa Safe Passage</a>, que coloca personas en las esquinas para vigilar a los estudiantes mientras van y vienen de la escuela, y probar un programa de transporte que podría usarse durante los aumentos de los federales. Eso podría incluir trabajar con organizaciones comunitarias de confianza para transportar a los estudiantes a la escuela o expandir las rutas de autobús, dice el informe.</p><p>Los grupos también sugieren que los comités de seguridad escolar compuestos por maestros y otro personal colaboren con grupos de padres que deseen involucrarse.</p><p>Finalmente, quieren que CPS intensifique su apoyo para estudiantes afectados por la actividad de inmigración federal. Dicen que CPS debería expandir los servicios de salud mental y dirigir a los estudiantes para recibir apoyo alimentario y económico “para que el miedo temporal no cause daños académicos a largo plazo”.</p><p>También dicen que CPS debería asegurarse de que los estudiantes tengan computadoras portátiles y una conexión a internet confiable para permitir algo de aprendizaje o tutoría remota si los estudiantes individuales enfrentan un verdadero riesgo de seguridad para asistir a la escuela en persona.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por</i> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: Deerfield beats undefeated Kaneland in OT to earn a spot in the 3A championship
entries.summary: <p>CHAMPAIGN—Deerfield used a picture of the State Farm Center floor as motivation all season. Perhaps all that time looking at the court helped <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/2026/02/27/chicago-sun-times-all-area-boys-basketball-team-2026" >All-Area senior Jake Pollack </a>develop some extra comfort when the dream became a reality.</p><p>Pollack owned the floor on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-boys-basketball-state-finals-scores-illinois" >Friday</a> in the Warriors’ 49-48 overtime win against Kaneland in the Class 3A semifinals.</p><p>The 6-9 Dartmouth recruit was an offensive and defensive force with multiple charges taken and five blocks, most of which came after picking up his third foul.</p><p>“I was pretty stupid at the start,” Pollack said. “I kept swiping with my hands. I stopped swiping. With the work I have put in, I am strong enough to play [Kaneland’s 6-9 Jeffrey Hassan] chest to chest. I gave him some trouble.”</p><p>Hassan and Pollack staged an excellent big-man battle. Pollack finished with 20 points, seven rebounds and four assists. Deerfield’s second-half offense primarily consisted of Pollack getting to the rim against Hassan and scoring or being fouled.</p><p>“They have been telling me all year to go to the rim,” Pollack said. “I got a few buckets early and got settled in. When they switched [Hassan] on me, I felt like I could get by and get to the rim. I got him in foul trouble which also helped.”</p><p>Hassan, one of the highest-rated recruits in the state’s junior class, finished with 10 points, 15 rebounds and three blocks.</p><p>“[Pollack] was the best player on the floor,” Deerfield senior Evan Nagler said. “Zero debate. When it is winning time, you go to your best player. That’s exactly what he did. He executed and pulled through for us.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-boys-basketball-state-finals-scores-illinois" >IHSA boys basketball state finals schedule and scores</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/2026/03/09/deerfield-high-school-basketball-ihsa-state" >Deerfield’s Champaign dreams come true in supersectional victory against St. Francis</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Nagler contributed 11 points and freshman Drew Schnurman made three three-pointers in the second quarter to finish with nine points.</p><p>Deerfield (25-11) led by 14 points with 3:09 left in the third quarter. Kaneland (35-1) closed the third with a 13-0 run to get back in the game. The Knights took a 40-39 lead on Jalen Carter’s three-pointer with 2:50 left in regulation.</p><p>Kaneland guard Marshawn Cocroft scored on a baseline drive with 10 seconds left to tie the game at 45. Nagler missed a potential game-winner on the next possession, and the game went to overtime.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure 2:54. Pollack scored twice on aggressive drives to the basket to provide the winning margin for Deerfield. Cocroft had a good look from three at the buzzer for Kaneland but missed.</p><p>“Our kids executed the game plan exceptionally well,” Warriors coach Dan McKendrick said. “The best part about our team is our heart and you saw that in overtime.”</p><p>Evan Frieders scored 10 for Kaneland, Carter added nine and Cocroft finished with eight points.</p><p>“Credit to Deerfield, they played well,” Knights coach Ernie Colombe said. “It was a great second half, a great high school basketball game and a great atmosphere. I’m just really proud of these guys.”</p><p>Deerfield will make its first state championship game appearance against the Leo vs. East St. Louis winner on Saturday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-ed0000" name="html-embed-module-ed0000"></a><blockquote class="tiktok-embed" cite="https://www.tiktok.com/@michaelsobriensports/video/7616792489663483166" data-video-id="7616792489663483166" style="max-width: 605px;min-width: 325px;" > <section> <a target="_blank" title="@michaelsobriensports" href="https://www.tiktok.com/@michaelsobriensports?refer=embed">@michaelsobriensports</a> Deerfield beat undefeated Kaneland 49-48 in OT in the IHSA Class 3A semifinals. <a title="basketball" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/basketball?refer=embed">#basketball</a> <a title="ihsa" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/ihsa?refer=embed">#ihsa</a> <a title="deerfield" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/deerfield?refer=embed">#deerfield</a> <a title="kaneland" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/kaneland?refer=embed">#kaneland</a> <a target="_blank" title="♬ original sound - michaelsobriensports" href="https://www.tiktok.com/music/original-sound-7616792442209127198?refer=embed">♬ original sound - michaelsobriensports</a> </section> </blockquote> <script async src="https://www.tiktok.com/embed.js"></script> </div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: Finance Committee authorizes $27M settlement stemming from deadly police chase
entries.summary: <p>A City Council committee on Friday agreed to pay <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/11/chicago-police-chase-settlement-27-million-dollars-finance-committee-englewood-crash" >$27 million </a>to the family of a mother of six killed in a high-speed police chase — nearly triple the amount awarded by a jury — amid warnings that new evidence in the case exposed taxpayers to a settlement “well over $100 million.”</p><p>The settlement authorized by the Finance Committee is poised for full Council approval next week, and would go to the family of Stacy Vaughn-Harrell.</p><p>The 47-year-old woman and her then 21-year-old daughter, Kimberlyn Myers, were driving home in June 2017 — after Myers sang at a performance in Indiana — when they were hit by a car that was fleeing police through a residential area in Englewood at a speed of roughly 50 mph.</p><p>Vaughn-Harrell was killed, and Myers suffered serious injuries, including a concussion, a lacerated liver, and a broken collarbone requiring a plate and five screws. Vaughn-Harrell left behind six children, three of whom were teenagers.</p><p>Before the chase, police had pulled over a white Kia they believed was present during a shooting, though they didn’t know if the shots came from the car, the family’s attorney said at the time. A passenger got out of the car when it was pulled over, then the Kia sped off.</p><p>Police chased the Kia in an unmarked car, with a marked car following, according to the family’s trial attorney, who contended this violated department policy requiring a marked car to lead a chase using both lights and sirens.</p><p>The Kia had run through four stop signs before crashing into Vaughn-Harrell’s car at an intersection.</p><p>Three years ago, a jury awarded <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/5/17/23727290/jury-awards-10-million-woman-killed-chicago-police-chase-crash-englewood-2017-stacy-vaughn-harrell" target="_blank" >$10 million</a> to the victim's family.</p><p>On Friday, Deputy Corporation Counsel Margaret Mendenhall-Casey cited six reasons to explain why an appellate court’s decision to order a new trial based on an improper closing argument and other legal violations turned into a proposed $27 million settlement, all but $7 million shouldered by Chicago taxpayers.</p><p>Chief among them is a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/12/woman-killed-police-chase-city-council-27-million-settlement" >post-crash video</a> that the “first jury did not see,” and the new trial judge has “already hinted he is inclined" to allow in a second trial, she said.</p><p>Lawyers for Vaughn-Harrell's family likely would use that video “ to argue that our officers were callous," Mendenhall-Casey said. "Video of Kimberlyn crawling over her mother’s dead body and falling to the ground while officers stood by and watched, the plaintiff will argue.” </p><p>Other factors that strengthen the family’s case include: likely testimony from all six of the victim’s children, only two of whom testified at the first trial; more detail about Myers' traumatic brain injury as well as testimony from her and her doctors; and the fact that the six surviving children lost not only their mother but their home-schooling teacher.</p><p>A second jury would also be permitted to hear more criticism of the police officers involved in the pursuit that was not allowed during the first trial, Mendenhall-Casey said. And a grieving family that did not seek compensation for pain and suffering would now likely seek those funds. </p><p>“As you can see, a second trial would have a substantially different and larger case presented to it on damages,” Mendenhall-Casey told the Finance Committee. “If this matter proceeds to a second trial, the plaintiff will ask the jury to award well over $100 million dollars.”</p><p>The City Council recently rejected an $8.25 million settlement in another deadly police pursuit case. But the city's managing deputy of litigation, John Hendricks, advised alderpersons not to roll the dice on this case. </p><p>“ We have a different case with new evidence. We have a different case with new witnesses. We have a different case with new and broader claims for damages. And we have a likelihood of more evidence coming in that was not allowed at the first trial based on judicial findings,” Hendricks said.</p><p>Citing a 2024 police pursuit case with a similar set of facts that culminated in a $79 million settlement, Hendricks said, “We have a new set of facts similar to that set of facts that resulted in what people sometimes refer to as a nuclear verdict.”</p><p>During the public comment period that preceded Friday’s hearing, the Finance Committee heard tearful testimony from Myers. </p><p>“I am the individual who crawled out of that car. Who was not assisted by no officer. I did not see one hand reach out for me,” Myers said. “Every day there is pain that we all go through. Today is just as hard as it was on June 24, [2017].” </p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Employee of Chicago cybersecurity firm brokered $75m in ransom payments after orchestrating hacks: feds
entries.summary: <p>Cybersecurity expert Angelo Martino was a wolf in sheep’s clothing, prosecutors say.</p><p>Martino, a 41-year-old employee of Chicago cybersecurity company DigitalMint, negotiated on behalf of companies hit with multimillion-dollar ransom demands after their computers were hacked.</p><p>But he actually helped orchestrate the hacks at the center of those same ransom schemes, prosecutors say. Martino and his associates are accused of extorting more than $75 million in ransoms from four companies and a nonprofit organization that he represented as a negotiator.</p><p>The victims included hospitality, retail, medical and financial services businesses. Two of the ransom payoffs were for more than $25 million, according to court records.</p><p>The feds have seized Martino’s properties in Florida, his vehicles and more than $9 million in cryptocurrency. A hearing is scheduled Thursday for Martino to consider entering a guilty plea.</p><p>Martino conspired with Kevin Tyler Martin, another ransom threat negotiator for DigitalMint, and Ryan Clifford Goldberg, an incident response manager for Sygnia Cybersecurity Services, according to prosecutors</p><p>Goldberg’s role in the scheme was to identify computer weaknesses and gain access to victims’ networks. Martin’s job was to identify victims, extract data from their computers and lock their networks, prosecutors say.</p><p>In December, Goldberg and Martin pleaded guilty to extortion and are scheduled to be sentenced April 30. Martino was referred to as an unnamed co-conspirator in that case.</p><h3><b>‘Ransomware’ called BlackCat</b></h3><p>Prosecutors say the scheme began in 2023.</p><p>Goldberg told FBI agents he got involved to get out of debt, according to court records.</p><p>Martino and his associates instructed companies to pay ransoms in exchange for a key to unlock encrypted data they’d hacked — along with a promise not to publish the victims’ stolen data, prosecutors say.</p><p>Martino and his associates allegedly hacked into victims’ computer networks using software called ALPHV BlackCat, which has been used in hundreds of attacks around the world.</p><p>The developers of ALPHV BlackCat provided the crew with access to their ransomware under what’s called an “affiliate” agreement, in which a portion of proceeds from a ransom payment is kicked back to the developers, according to prosecutors.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3dac72b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/980x550+111+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fcf%2F21e250d84f23a2a6d06436adaaab%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-13-at-5-11-54-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/847e445/2147483647/strip/true/crop/980x550+111+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fcf%2F21e250d84f23a2a6d06436adaaab%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-13-at-5-11-54-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3dac72b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/980x550+111+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fcf%2F21e250d84f23a2a6d06436adaaab%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-13-at-5-11-54-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A back-and-forth conversation between a hacked cosmetic surgery office in Los Angeles and an alleged ransomware crew in 2023. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court, Miami</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Martino and Martin have both been fired by DigitalMint, and Goldberg no longer works for Sygnia.</p><p>In a statement, DigitalMint CEO Jonathan Solomon said the Justice Department informed the company of the allegations in April 2025, and Martino "was terminated the next day." Solomon said the company reviewed Martino's work and found "no evidence of other criminal conduct beyond what is reflected in the charging documents."</p><p>“DigitalMint has fully cooperated with law enforcement from the outset and does not expect further charges," he said. "While no organization can completely eliminate insider risk, we take incidents like this extremely seriously and have strengthened safeguards and internal controls to further reduce the likelihood of similar conduct.”</p><h3><b>‘More bad guys out there’</b></h3><h3></h3><p>According to TRM Labs — a company that helps financial institutions and government agencies like the FBI investigate cryptocurrency-related fraud — ransom attacks rose last year, along with the spread of various types of ransomware.</p><p>“There's just more people [and] more entities being victimized because there's more bad guys out there looking to unfortunately take advantage of vulnerabilities and cybersecurity to take innocent people's money, to put it bluntly,” Alek Langford, a TRM Labs analyst, told the Chicago Sun-Times earlier this year.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/02/crytpo-cryptocurrency-crime-chicago-digital-mint-ransom-ransomware-hack" target="_blank" >Chicago firm that resolves ransomware attacks had rogue workers carrying out their own hacks, FBI says</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Tom Schuba

entries.title: Where to celebrate St. Patrick's Day weekend in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>With its rich Irish American heritage, Chicago has long been an iconic site for St. Patrick’s Day celebrations which start long before the official holiday Tuesday.</p><p>Things kick off Saturday morning, when the Chicago Plumbers Local 130 will deploy a team on a boat to dye the Chicago River green, as it has every year since 1962. The St. Patrick’s Day Parade then steps off in the afternoon followed by another parade on the Far Southwest Side.</p><p>That’s all amidst various special deals at Irish pubs and restaurants across the city and plenty of bar crawls planned for Saturday and Sunday.</p><p>Here are some options to celebrate St. Patrick’s Day in Chicago:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-308bbb22-1f29-11f1-9cbb-db22d8e463ca"><li><b>8 a.m. Saturday:</b> The Hubbard Street Goes Green street festival in the 100 block of West Hubbard Street in River North features more than 20 DJs and bands, food, drinks and access to several bars.</li><li><b>10 a.m. Saturday:</b> Chicago River Dyeing begins on the Chicago River between Columbus Drive and Orleans Street.</li><li><b>Noon Saturday:</b> Archer Avenue St. Patrick’s Day parade steps off at Archer and South Oak Park avenues, heading east to Archer and Merrimac Avenue.</li><li><b>12:15 p.m. Saturday:</b> The city's St. Patrick’s Day Parade steps off at Columbus and Balbo drives, heading north to Monroe Street.</li><li><b>11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Sunday:</b> St. Patrick’s Day at Gallagher Way, 3635 N. Clark St., features Irish dancers, music, drinks and crafts.</li><li><b>Noon Sunday:</b> The South Side Irish Parade steps off at 103rd Street and Western Avenue, marching south to 115th Street and Western Avenue.</li><li><b>12:30 p.m. Saturday:</b> The Irish American Heritage Center, 4626 N. Knox Ave., hosts a two-day St. Patrick’s Day festival featuring bands, Irish dancers, cultural performances, crafts, games and a kids area. Sunday’s festival begins at 1 p.m.</li><li><b>Noon Sunday:</b> The Northwest Side Irish Parade steps off at Onahan School, 6634 W. Raven St., heading south on Neola Avenue to Northwest Highway and north to Harlem Avenue.</li><li><b>2 p.m. Tuesday:</b> The Irish American Heritage Center, 4626 N. Knox Ave., hosts a St. Patrick’s Day party with live music, dancing, food and drinks.</li></ul>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Mayor Brandon Johnson's choice to head COPA vows to rebuild police and public's trust in the oversight agency
entries.summary: <p>When a nationwide search ended with the nomination of the Civilian Office of Police Accountability's acting chief administrator as the agency's permanent chief, it looked like a repeat of a familiar Chicago story, in which an insider who doesn't make waves gets retained. </p><p>But 25-year-veteran<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2026/01/29/chicago-police-oversight-commission-names-new-copa-boss" > LaKenya White</a> used her confirmation hearing Friday to dispel the notion that she got the job in order to maintain the status quo.</p><p>White told the Police and Fire Committee she intends to do plenty of things differently at an agency that officers view as a deck stacked against them — and that everyday Chicagoans don’t trust to punish police wrongdoing. </p><p>“I have seen the tears of a mother who has lost her child in an officer-involved shooting... I have seen the tears of an officer who had to make split-second decisions in order to save his own life,” White told the committee. “I’m not here to make friends… I am here to educate. I’m here to foster a better working relationship. But I’m also here to be fair, to be truthful. And if that gets me not liked, then so be it.” </p><p>COPA is the agency responsible for making disciplinary recommendations after investigating officer-involved shootings and deaths, serious injuries of individuals in Chicago Police Department custody, or serious allegations of misconduct, such as excessive force, sexual misconduct and bias-based verbal abuse.</p><p>The Police and Fire Committee supported White's confirmation, which now goes to the full City Council Wednesday for a final vote.</p><p>White said the agency “got a bad rep from different cases and it’s unfortunate.” Her goal is to change that perception, in part by ensuring that her staff has the training it needs to conduct fair, thorough and timely investigations, and is “compassionate to officers and the job that they do.”</p><p>She appeared to be referring to public trust shattered by the police shootings of Rekia Boyd in 2012 and Laquan McDonald in 2014, as well as the city's decision to withhold the McDonald shooting video until a court ordered then-Mayor Rahm Emanuel to release it.</p><p>Boyd was 22 when she was shot by an off-duty police officer. The officer, Dante Servin, was later acquitted, and that verdict became a rallying point for the #SayHerName movement, and a reckoning over how the city values the lives of Black women and girls.</p><p>White was the chief investigator in the Boyd case. She told alderpersons the case will “forever resonate with me.” To help rebuild public trust, she said she plans to create a youth commission and do outreach to Chicago Public Schools and City Colleges. </p><p>During Friday's confirmation hearing, South Side Ald. Greg Mitchell (7th) talked about the rise in activism over his twelve years in the City Council tailor-made to “sway opinion.” He asked White how that heightened level of activism would “affect you in… making decisions in your job?” </p><p>White said she was “not affected by activism.”</p><p>“Some days, I’m going to be considered pro-police and other days I’m going to be anti-police. But this is the nature of the beast. This is what I signed up for,” she said.</p><p>Chicago’s police accountability agency has been without a permanent leader since February, 2025 when Chief Administrator Andrea Kersten resigned to avoid a potentially embarrassing vote of no-confidence.</p><p>Kersten faced strong opposition throughout her tenure as COPA chief. Before she was appointed, she came under fire for recommending that slain police officer Ella French be suspended for her role in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/8/24/23845256/anjanette-young-recalls-decision-to-allow-footage-of-botched-2019-raid-her-home-to-be-released" target="_blank" >raid of social worker Anjanette Young’s home.</a></p><p>Kersten apologized for the “heartache, frustration and just flat-out anger” she had caused French’s grieving family, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2022/2/23/22948026/chicago-city-council-gang-forfeiture-brickyard-dragging-settlement-copa-administrator-andrea-kersten" target="_blank" >and was confirmed a month later</a> — but the pushback continued.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/08/30/officials-police-oversight-agency-copa-dismissed-retaliation-bias-against-police" target="_blank" >Two former COPA officials sued the city,</a> claiming they were retaliated against for complaining about Kersten’s leadership. </p><p>The city’s largest police union also sued the agency under Kersten, claiming it had carried out “biased and unfair investigations.” One of the former officials still has a pending case in court — the other suits have been dismissed.</p><p>White has worked at previous iterations of the police oversight agency, including the Office of Professional Standards in 2000, and the Independent Police Review Authority in 2007, where she handled a number of officer-involved shootings. </p><p>She joined COPA upon its creation in 2017, and eventually became the agency’s Director of Investigations for Intake, overseeing all incoming complaints.</p><p>“I love what I do and I want to showcase that to you all, to the community and to officers as well. Because we do care about officers’ perceptions of COPA. And I want to change that narrative.” White said Friday. She vowed to "put boots on the ground" in Chicago neighborhoods to "foster a better working relationship with the community and police, and educate the community about COPA and the broader system of police accountability.”</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Cubs' Nico Hoerner taking different approach to generating power
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — When <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> second baseman Nico Hoerner roped a home run to left-center field this week for his first of the spring, it wasn’t because he bulked up over the offseason or tweaked his swing for more power.</p><p>“A lot of it is what you swing at pitch-wise,” Hoerner said in a recent conversation with the Sun-Times. “So having a better understanding of not just what I make contact with, but what I drive. And if you can put more balls in play in that bucket throughout the season, then I like my chances to slug more, whether it's home runs or extra-base hits in general.”</p><p>Hoerner’s quest for power has gone through many evolutions. It’s been a spring-training storyline year after year, as he’s sought to balance his natural talents with that clear area for improvement.</p><p>Although he is still quick to bring up a desire to drive the ball more consistently, over the last year, Hoerner’s appreciation for his contact ability has come through more. It may sound counter-intuitive, but that shift may be the key to unlocking Hoerner’s power.</p><p>“Just in the three years that I’ve been here, I’ve seen him come into spring with some different goals,” manager Craig Counsell said. “And now I think he sits in a place where he understands himself better and how to achieve those goals. Instead of just saying, ‘I want to hit for more power,’ it’s more understanding how his body works around that goal.”</p><p>Hoerner agreed with Counsell’s assessment.</p><p>“When it comes to hitting for power, people have always told me, ‘Get your swing off, and do damage, and take chances,’” Hoerner said. “And those are all well-intentioned. I think there's validity to that. But those are phrases that you have to make sense of yourself.”</p><p>Hoerner now sees the swings that he thought were more powerful as just bigger and more out of control.</p><p>“They were just kind of sloppy for me,” he said, “and don't really embrace my skill sets of being a shorter, explosive mover.”</p><p>Hoerner harnessed that explosive movement in the second half of the season last year, hitting all seven of his home runs after mid-June, as he became further removed from the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2024/10/21/cubs-nico-hoerner-undergoes-flexor-tendon-surgery" >forearm surgery he underwent the previous offseason</a><b>. </b></p><p>Hoerner also finished the season with the second-best batting average (.297) in the National League. Then in the postseason, he showed just how valuable his contact-centric skillset can be.</p><p>“It was really special to watch him in that element,” left fielder Ian Happ said. “And the competitiveness and his skill set plays so well in those situations because he's so good at moving the ball forward and putting together a really competitive at-bat.”</p><p>Hoerner led Cubs lineup regulars in the postseason with a .419 batting average. He recorded a double, home run and four RBI.</p><p>“Results in a small sample like that can come and go,” Hoerner said. “But obviously we faced such a high level of pitching between San Diego and Milwaukee, and especially in their bullpens, you're not facing pitchers usually more than once or twice. It was a very challenging setting. So it meant a lot to me, both mentally and skillset wise, to be able to just have quality at-bats.”</p><p>Postseason pitching is bound to be staunch this year too, as the Cubs shoot for a deeper playoff run. And with Hoerner heading to free agency for the first time in his career, barring an extension, that could be his last hurrah with the team that drafted him in 2018.</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel entries.author: Maddie Lee

entries.title: White Sox outfielder Brooks Baldwin sidelined by right elbow inflammation
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. —<b> Brooks Baldwin</b>’s injury is a hit to an already thin outfield group for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a>.</p><p>Baldwin hasn’t played since March 6 because of right elbow inflammation. He said he felt the injury while throwing to bases recently.</p><p>“Talking to the trainers, we got an image, we got everything done,” Baldwin said Friday. “But a lot of soreness in there, [we're trying] to get it to calm down and take it day to day and see where we’re at.”</p><p>The severity of the injury has yet to be determined. The Sox are checking in on Baldwin daily, but they haven't made any decision regarding Opening Day.</p><p>With the infield largely set, Baldwin was going to play more exclusively in the outfield. The Sox hope that with more familiarity and consistency, Baldwin can deliver a better defensive performance than he did last season. He has the athleticism for either corner outfield spot, but he struggled with reads.</p><p>At the plate, Baldwin was building on his success from last season. He batted .253 with a .769 OPS and a wRC+ of 112. Baldwin found success when he reverted to a more open batting stance that he utilized at UNC Wilmington. The wider stance allowed Baldwin to keep his head still.</p><p>“I thought at-bats were going good [in spring training],” Baldwin said. “I saw the ball well. Defensively, [I] was getting good jumps in the outfield and tracking balls down pretty well.”</p><p>Baldwin said he dealt with elbow soreness last season. He doesn’t feel that anything is torn, but he’s going to consult with more specialists for a more definitive ruling.</p><p>If Baldwin is forced to miss time, the Sox’ outfield decision becomes dicier. <b>Tristan Peters </b>and<b> Derek Hill</b> are both glove-first outfielders who struggle at the plate — Hill is a better defender — and both are already on the 40-man roster. Manager Will Venable has praised Hill’s “elite defense,” and said he’s a player who is still coming into his own.</p><p><b>Jarred Kelenic</b> — who started Friday's game in center field and got a base hit — brings more upside as a former top prospect with more pop in his bat.</p><p>Aside from the rotation, the outfield is the most interesting roster battle as the start of the season nears.</p><h3><b>Espresso time</b></h3><p>Fresh off his appearance with Team Italy, catcher<b> Kyle Teel</b> wants an espresso machine in the Sox dugout.</p><p>Italy had one at the 2023 WBC and continued the tradition this year. Royals first baseman and Italy captain <b>Vinnie Pasquantino</b> came up with the idea of celebrating each home run with a shot of espresso.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Espresso celebrations are back for Italy! 🤌 <a href="https://t.co/DiLLQsVwi6">pic.twitter.com/DiLLQsVwi6</a></p>— FOX Sports: MLB (@MLBONFOX) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLBONFOX/status/2030376030322729452?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Teel said he brought the idea up to the club and that it’s a “good reward system.”</p><p>“Whatever gets the guys going,” manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. “If it’s an espresso in there, it’s fine by me.”</p><h3><b>White Sox 4, Cubs 2</b></h3><p>• Right-hander <b>Davis Martin</b> threw four scoreless innings, striking out four and allowing one hit with no walks.</p><p>• Catcher <b>Korey Lee</b> hit a solo homer in the fifth inning.</p><p>• Outfielder <b>Everson Peiera</b> hit his first home run of spring training in the sixth inning.</p><p><b>On deck:</b> Dodgers vs. White Sox, 1:05 p.m., Saturday, Glendale, Justin Wrobleski vs. Mike Vasil.</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005"
